- **Content value:** Obituaries, marriage notices, legal notices, and — especially significant for an anthracite coal county — mine accident and inquest reports.

### Immigration and naturalization

- **Naturalization records:** IGN 007936750 (1849–1915, 298 images, browse-only) is the primary digitized resource for local naturalizations. The FamilySearch Research Guidance wiki confirms pre-1906 naturalizations can appear in any court of record.
- **Passenger lists:** Nearest major ports of entry were Philadelphia and New York. NARA holds arrival manifests; FamilySearch catalog covers multiple passenger list collections.
- **Note:** When researching immigrant families, examine the full passenger manifest for all family members — infants and children traveling with parents are listed.

### Tax records

- **Available externally (pre-period):** Ancestry.com holds "Pennsylvania, U.S., Tax and Exoneration Lists, 1768–1801" (per FamilySearch external links) — this predates both Schuylkill County's formation (1811) and the target period.
- **1840–1880 tax records:** No digitized county tax or assessment volume for 1840–1880 was returned by volume_search. County assessment books (held at Schuylkill County Courthouse or Pennsylvania State Archives) can substitute for missing census years.

---

## Repository guide

### Online repositories

| Repository | Collection / Resource | Access |
|---|---|---|
| FamilySearch | Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985 (collection 1401638; 1,245,830 records, indexed + images) | Free |
| FamilySearch | Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994 (collection 1417683; 2,304,118 images, browse) | Free |
| FamilySearch | Schuylkill Co. Church Records 1842–1895 — IGN 007936751, 187 images, browse-only, English+German | Free |
| FamilySearch | Schuylkill Co. Probate Records 1851–1930 — IGN 007936749, 412 images, browse-only | Free |
| FamilySearch | Schuylkill Co. Naturalization Records 1849–1915 — IGN 007936750, 298 images, browse-only | Free |
| FamilySearch | Schuylkill Co. Deed Records 1815–1900 — IGN 007936752, 543 images, 22% indexed | Free |
| FamilySearch | Schuylkill Co. Marriage Records 1870–1910 — IGN 007936753, 94 images, browse-only | Free |
| Ancestry.com | 1850 United States Federal Census — Schuylkill County | Subscription |

### Physical repositories

| Repository | Holdings | Access method |
|---|---|---|
| Schuylkill County Courthouse, Pottsville PA | Deeds, probate, court, marriage records (originals) | In-person / written request |
| Pennsylvania State Archives, Harrisburg PA | State-level records, military, state census | In-person / online catalog |
| National Archives (NARA), Philadelphia region | Federal census, military service/pension, naturalization | In-person / NARA online |
| Schuylkill County Historical Society, Pottsville PA | Local manuscripts, newspapers, cemetery surveys, genealogical collections | In-person |
| Denominational archives | Church registers not on FamilySearch | Contact each denomination |

---

## Records NOT online

- **Marriage records 1840–1869:** The digitized marriage volume begins 1870. Pre-1870 marriage records require courthouse access or church records.
- **Probate records 1840–1850:** The digitized probate volume begins 1851. Earlier probate files require courthouse or physical archive access.
- **Tax / assessment records 1840–1880:** No digitized county tax volume was returned — physical access at courthouse or Pennsylvania State Archives required.
- **Newspaper archives:** No digitized Schuylkill County newspaper collection was returned — check Chronicling America and Pennsylvania State Library.
- **Cemetery records:** No FamilySearch-curated cemetery collection returned — rely on FindAGrave, BillionGraves, and local society transcriptions.

---

## Known record losses

No specific courthouse fires, floods, or catastrophic record losses for Schuylkill County were reported in tool data for this survey. Verify current holdings and any known losses with the Schuylkill County Courthouse and the Pennsylvania State Archives before assuming a record class is absent.

---

## Research tips

1. **Church records before civil registration:** Verify Pennsylvania's statewide vital registration start date (the FamilySearch wiki advises many U.S. states did not require it until the early 1900s). For 1840–1880, church registers are the primary source for births, marriages, and burials. The FamilySearch church records volume (IGN 007936751, 1842–1895) covers the full target period but must be browsed image by image.
2. **German-language records:** The church records volume contains German-language entries. Schuylkill County had significant German-heritage communities. Be prepared to read or translate 19th-century German script.
3. **Browse-only is the rule:** Of the 5 digitized volumes on FamilySearch, 4 are 0% indexed and must be browsed without any name search. Plan substantial browsing time, especially for probate (412 images), naturalization (298 images), and church (187 images) records.
4. **Marriage record gap 1840–1869:** The digitized marriage volume starts in 1870. For earlier marriages, search church records (IGN 007936751) and the indexed Pennsylvania Church and Town Records collection (1401638).
5. **Pre-1811 ancestry:** For ancestors in this geographic area before 1811, records are held by the predecessor county or counties. Identify the parent jurisdiction and search there.
6. **Naturalization in any court:** Before 1906, immigrants could naturalize in any court of record. If no record appears in IGN 007936750, check federal courts and neighboring county courts.
7. **FamilySearch Catalog:** Consult catalog.familysearch.org by county to find additional Schuylkill County microfilm or digital holdings not captured in this survey.
8. **FamilySearch Pennsylvania wiki:** The wiki tool returned only generic content during this survey. Consult https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Pennsylvania_Genealogy directly for state-specific research strategies, confirmed vital registration dates, and repository contacts.

# Locality Guide: Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania — 1840s–1860s

## Jurisdictional Context

**Schuylkill County** was formed in **1811** from Berks and Northampton counties. The county seat is **Pottsville**. It experienced explosive population growth in the 1840s–1860s driven by anthracite coal mining, drawing heavy Welsh, Irish (especially post-Famine, 1845–1852), and German immigrant populations. Records filed during this period were created at the **county level** — there was no statewide vital registration in Pennsylvania until 1906.

No boundary changes split the county during the target period; all records trace to a single jurisdictional trail.

---

## Record-by-Record Survey

### 1. 🟢 Federal Census — INDEXED + IMAGES

| Detail | Value |
|---|---|
| FamilySearch Volume | 007621224 |
| Coverage | 1850–1860 (Schuylkill County) |
| Images | 412 |
| Indexing | **96% name-indexed** |
| Full-text searchable | Yes |
| Language | English |
| Access | Search directly on FamilySearch |

The 1850 and 1860 federal censuses for Schuylkill County are **well-indexed and fully searchable** — the strongest online record set for this period. The **1850 census** is also available on **Ancestry.com** ([direct link](https://www.ancestry.com/search/collections/1276/)). Both censuses include mortality schedules (deaths in the year preceding enumeration, 1850 and 1860) which are part of the broader census returns.

> ⚠️ **Gap — 1840 Census:** The 1840 Federal Census for Schuylkill County did not appear in FamilySearch's digitized volume results for this time window. Researchers should check the FamilySearch catalog directly or consult microfilm holdings at NARA/state archives for the 1840 enumeration.

---

### 2. 🟡 Probate Records — FULL-TEXT SEARCHABLE, NOT NAME-INDEXED

| Detail | Value |
|---|---|
| FamilySearch Volume | 004812990 |
| Coverage | 1845–1880 (Schuylkill County) |
| Images | 286 |
| Indexing | **4% name-indexed** — very limited |
| Full-text searchable | Yes |
| Language | English |
| FamilySearch Collection | Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994 (ID: 1417683; 2,304,118 images) |
| Access | Full-text search on FamilySearch; browsable image-by-image |

Probate records (wills, inventories, letters of administration, guardianship files) for 1845–1880 are digitized but **only 4% are name-indexed**. You cannot rely on a standard name search to surface these records. Use FamilySearch's **full-text search** function on the collection, or plan to browse images directly. The county-level volume sits within the larger Pennsylvania Probate Records collection.

---

### 3. 🔴 Church Records (Lutheran) — BROWSE-ONLY, BILINGUAL

| Detail | Value |
|---|---|
| FamilySearch Volume | 004288771 |
| Coverage | 1840–1875 (Schuylkill County) |
| Images | 198 |
| Indexing | **0% name-indexed** |
| Full-text searchable | **No** |
| Language | **English AND German** |
| FamilySearch Collection | Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985 (ID: 1401638; 1,245,830 records, 607,220 images) |
| Access | **Browse image-by-image only — no search** |

This digitized Lutheran register volume must be **browsed page by page** with no name-search capability whatsoever. It is also bilingual — entries may be in **German**, requiring the researcher to read the original language. Since Pennsylvania had no civil vital registration before 1906, **church registers are the primary source for births, baptisms, marriages, and burials** in this period. Lutheran, Reformed, and Catholic parishes were prominent in Schuylkill County given its German and Irish immigrant communities. Other denominations' registers (Catholic, Welsh Calvinist Methodist, Episcopal) are not represented in the volume results — their records may exist only in **physical repositories** (local churches, denominational archives, county historical society) or on undigitized microfilm.

---

### 4. Vital Records — NO CIVIL REGISTRATION IN THIS PERIOD

Pennsylvania **did not require statewide registration of births and deaths until 1906**. For the 1840s–1860s, church registers (see above) are the **only systematic source** for vital events. Marriage licenses were issued at the county level; marriage bonds and register books are held at the **Schuylkill County Courthouse** and at the **Pennsylvania State Archives** in Harrisburg. No digitized county marriage collection for this period appeared in FamilySearch's volume results — treat marriage records as **physical/microfilm only** until confirmed otherwise.

---

### 5. Land & Tax Records — PHYSICAL/MICROFILM

No digitized FamilySearch volumes for Schuylkill County land or tax records appeared in the target window. The FamilySearch external links search returned one Ancestry collection for Pennsylvania Tax and Exoneration Lists, but it covers **1768–1801 only** — entirely outside the 1840s–1860s target. For this period:

- **Deed books** are held at the **Schuylkill County Recorder of Deeds**, Pottsville
- **Annual county tax assessment lists** are held at the **Pennsylvania State Archives** (Record Group 4) — essential for locating individuals between censuses
- **Original land warrants and patents** are at the **Pennsylvania State Archives**

These records are likely on microfilm at FamilySearch Family History Centers but did not surface as digitized online volumes.

---

### 6. Court Records & Naturalizations — PHYSICAL/MICROFILM

Pre-1906 naturalizations in Pennsylvania could be filed in **any court of record** — the Court of Common Pleas, U.S. District Court, or other courts. Given Schuylkill County's massive immigrant influx in the 1840s–1860s, naturalization records here can be substantial and varied. Records are held at:

- **Schuylkill County Courthouse** (Court of Common Pleas naturalization dockets)
- **NARA Philadelphia** (federal court naturalizations)

No digitized naturalization volume for Schuylkill County appeared in FamilySearch results — treat as **physical or undigitized microfilm**. Court records (civil suits, criminal cases, orphans' court/guardianship) are similarly at the county courthouse.

---

### 7. Military Records — NATIONAL ARCHIVES

The target period spans the **Mexican-American War (1846–1848)** and the **Civil War (1861–1865)**. Key record sets:

- **Civil War service records and pension files** — NARA (available on Fold3/Ancestry)
- **Civil War draft registrations (1863)** — NARA; available on FamilySearch and Ancestry
- **Pennsylvania adjutant general records** — Pennsylvania State Archives

No Schuylkill County-specific military volumes appeared in FamilySearch's digitized results for this window, but broader Pennsylvania and federal military collections on Ancestry and Fold3 cover this period.

---

## Repositories

| Repository | Holdings Relevant to 1840s–1860s |
|---|---|
| **FamilySearch.org** | Census (1850–1860, indexed), probate volumes (full-text), Lutheran church register (browse-only) |
| **Ancestry.com** | 1850 U.S. Federal Census – Schuylkill County (indexed) |
| **Schuylkill County Courthouse, Pottsville** | Deeds, marriage licenses, naturalization dockets, court records |
| **Pennsylvania State Archives, Harrisburg** | Tax lists, land records, state-level military records |
| **NARA Philadelphia** | Federal court naturalizations, federal census originals |
| **NARA Washington/Annex** | Civil War service & pension records |
| **Schuylkill County Historical Society, Pottsville** | Local newspapers (Miners' Journal, est. 1825), cemetery records, compiled genealogies |
| **Denominational archives** | Catholic, Lutheran, Reformed, Methodist church registers not yet digitized |

---

## Key Research Tips & Quirks

1. **No civil vital registration before 1906** — church records are the only systematic source for births, marriages, and deaths. Identify the family's denomination early; don't assume Lutheran.
2. **Lutheran church register (vol. 004288771) is browse-only and bilingual English/German** — budget time to page through 198 images manually; bring German reading ability or engage a translator.
3. **Probate records are only 4% name-indexed** — use full-text search on FamilySearch rather than the standard name-search interface, or browse the 286-image volume directly.
4. **Heavy immigrant community** — Welsh, Irish, and German families arrived in large numbers 1840s–1860s. Naturalization records are critical but appear to be physical-only; check both the county courthouse and NARA Philadelphia.
5. **Miners' Journal (Pottsville, est. 1825)** is a key local newspaper for this period and may contain birth, marriage, and death notices as well as mining accident reports. Check the Schuylkill County Historical Society for access.
6. **Tax assessment lists** at the Pennsylvania State Archives are essential for tracking individuals between the 1850 and 1860 censuses — especially recent immigrants not yet appearing in land or probate records.
7. **FamilySearch curates very few third-party links** for Schuylkill County in this period (only 2 found, one outside the target window). Do not rely solely on FamilySearch's external links — consult Ancestry, Fold3, and Newspapers.com independently.
8. **1840 Census gap** — the 1840 Federal Census did not appear in FamilySearch's digitized volumes for this county; verify availability through the FamilySearch catalog or NARA.

---

## Digitization Summary

| Record Type | Period Available | FamilySearch Access | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Federal Census 1850 & 1860 | 1850, 1860 | ✅ Indexed + images | 96% indexed; also on Ancestry |
| Federal Census 1840 | 1840 | ⚠️ Gap in tools — verify | Not found in volume results |
| Probate Records | 1845–1880 | 🟡 Full-text searchable, not name-indexed | 4% indexed; 286 images |
| Lutheran Church Records | 1840–1875 | 🔴 Browse-only, bilingual | 0% indexed; English & German |
| Other Church Records | 1840–1870 | 📦 Physical/microfilm | Catholic, Methodist, Welsh denominations |
| Vital Records (civil) | N/A | ❌ Does not exist | PA statewide registration began 1906 |
| Marriage Records | 1840–1870 | 📦 Physical — county courthouse | |
| Land & Deed Records | 1840–1870 | 📦 Physical — county courthouse & PA State Archives | |
| Tax Assessments | 1840–1870 | 📦 Physical — PA State Archives | |
| Naturalizations | 1840–1870 | 📦 Physical — courthouse & NARA | |
| Civil War Military | 1861–1865 | ✅ Available on Ancestry/Fold3/NARA | Service records, pensions, draft regs |

# Locality Guide: Alabama Region, Pre-Statehood (circa 1780–1819)

## Jurisdiction Overview

- **Formed:** Alabama Territory created 1817 from the eastern portion of the Mississippi Territory; achieved statehood 3 December 1819 as the 27th state
- **Administrative centers:** Washington (territorial capital, 1817–1819); St. Stephens was the major settlement in the Alabama area ca. 1800
- **Parent jurisdiction at ca. 1800:** Mississippi Territory (established 7 April 1798); the Mobile district specifically remained under **Spanish colonial administration** until U.S. forces took Fort Charlotte in April 1813
- **Population during period:** No fixture population data was returned by the tools. Wikipedia notes settlement was concentrated around Mobile Bay (French-founded 1702) and expanded rapidly after the Creek War of 1813–1814, with migrants arriving from the Carolinas, Virginia, and Georgia
- **Economy:** Subsistence farming, cotton cultivation beginning in the early 1800s, fur trade, riverine commerce
- **Ethnic composition:** Creek and Cherokee nations occupied much of the interior; European settlers (Anglo-American, Scots-Irish) settling along rivers; French and Spanish Creole communities in Mobile; enslaved African Americans present throughout
- **Religious denominations:** Roman Catholic (Mobile area, French/Spanish heritage); Methodist and Baptist frontier congregations; Presbyterian missions to Native peoples
- **Key historical events:** Formation of Mississippi Territory (1798); U.S. acquisition of Mobile from Spain (1813); Creek War / Battle of Horseshoe Bend (1813–1814); formation of Alabama Territory (1817); Alabama statehood (1819)

---

## Boundary Changes — Critical for Research

This is the most important context for this period: **the Alabama region passed through three separate governing jurisdictions between ca. 1780 and 1819**, and records were created by each:

| Period | Jurisdiction | Record Authority |
|---|---|---|
| 1702–1763 | French Colony (Mobile area) | French colonial administration |
| 1763–1780 | British West Florida | British colonial administration |
| 1780–1813 | Spanish West Florida (Mobile district) | Spanish colonial administration |
| 1798–1817 | Mississippi Territory (inland areas) | U.S. Territorial government |
| 1817–1819 | Alabama Territory | U.S. Territorial government |
| 1819– | State of Alabama | Alabama state government |

> ⚠️ **Searcher alert:** Records for the pre-1817 inland Alabama area are filed under **Mississippi** (not Alabama) in FamilySearch, because the Mississippi Territory collection scope rolls up under the successor state. Search Mississippi-labeled collections for ancestors in the Alabama portion of the territory before 1817.

---

## Available Record Types

### Vital Records (Civil Registration)
- **Start date:** Alabama did not begin statewide civil registration of births and deaths until **1908**. For the ca. 1800 period, there are **no government-issued birth or death certificates whatsoever**.
- **Marriages:** County marriage records begin upon county formation — most Alabama counties did not exist in 1800. The FamilySearch collection **Alabama, County Marriages, 1809–1950** (collection 1598408; 612,345 records, 306,172 images) is the earliest indexed county-level series.
- **Pre-registration alternatives:** Church registers (baptism, marriage, burial) are the primary substitute. See Church Records below.

### Church Records
- **Catholic (Mobile area):** Roman Catholic sacramental registers (baptisms, marriages, burials) survive from the French and Spanish periods at Mobile. These are among the very earliest records for any person in present-day Alabama. Records are in **French and Spanish** — the researcher must read the original languages.
- **Protestant frontier churches:** Methodist and Baptist congregations established in the Mississippi Territory period. The FamilySearch collection **Alabama, Church Records, 1803–1950** (collection 1986396; 156,789 records, 78,394 images) covers this period from 1803. Similarly, **Mississippi, Church Records, 1800–1950** (collection 2034891; 87,654 records, 43,827 images) may cover congregations that straddled the territorial boundary.
- **Where held:** Parish-level records remain with individual churches or their diocesan archives. The Mobile Catholic Archdiocese Archives holds Mobile-area registers. FamilySearch microfilm copies of many church registers are browsable online or at the Family History Library.
- **Language:** Catholic records from the colonial era are in Latin, French, or Spanish.

### Census Records
- **Federal census:** The first federal census to enumerate the Alabama area was **1810** (as part of Mississippi Territory). The 1800 federal census **did not cover the Alabama area**. The 1810 census is indexed in FamilySearch collection **Alabama, Compiled Census and Census Substitutes Index, 1810–1890** (collection 2016756; 287,634 index entries, no images on FamilySearch) and also in **Mississippi, Compiled Census and Census Substitutes, 1792–1890** (collection 1927171; 198,340 records).
- **Territorial censuses:** Mississippi territorial censuses of **1801, 1805, 1808, and 1810** enumerated settlers in present-day Alabama. According to the FamilySearch Wiki (Alabama Genealogy article), these are held at the **Alabama Department of Archives and History (ADAH)** in Montgomery — they are **not available online** and require an in-person visit or records request.
- **Tax lists as census substitutes:** Territorial-era tax lists (see Tax Records below) effectively substitute for the missing 1800 census.

### Probate and Court Records
- **Probate:** The FamilySearch collection **Alabama, Probate Records, 1809–1985** (collection 1537764; 438,210 records, 219,105 images) begins at county formation. Pre-county territorial probate was handled by the Mississippi Territory courts.
- **Territorial court records:** The FamilySearch collection **Mississippi, County Court Records, 1799–1920** (collection 1855293; 245,670 records, 122,835 images) covers the territorial court system that governed the Alabama area before 1817. Search this collection for pre-statehood court filings.
- **Access:** Both collections have images on FamilySearch; check individual record volumes for searchability levels.

### Land Records
- **System:** Federal-land state — land was distributed by the U.S. General Land Office via cash sale, warrant, and patent after survey. This is the **single most important record type for this period**, because land transactions are often the first federal documentation of a settler.
- **First land office:** The St. Stephens Land Office (opened 1803) and the Huntsville Land Office (opened 1809) were the earliest in present-day Alabama.
- **Where held online:** The **BLM General Land Office Records** website (glorecords.blm.gov) holds federal land patents and is free to search. FamilySearch collection **Mississippi, Territorial and State Land Records, 1798–1907** (collection 1919694; 312,450 records, 156,225 images) covers the territorial-era land records.
- **Spanish land grants (Mobile area):** Spanish-era land grants in the Mobile district are a separate track. Copies are held at the **Library of Congress** and the **Mobile Municipal Archives**.
- **British grants:** British West Florida-era grants (1763–1780) for lands that became Alabama exist in British colonial archives; copies are at the Library of Congress and state archives.

### Military Records
- **Relevant conflicts:** Creek War (1813–1814), War of 1812 (overlapping), earlier militia service in the territorial period
- **Record types:** Militia muster rolls, pension files, bounty land warrant applications. Post-1820 pension applications often contain retrospective biographical detail covering the 1800 period.
- **Where held:** Pension and bounty-land files at the **National Archives** (NARA), Washington, D.C. Fold3 (curated external link confirmed) indexes many military pension and service files. State militia records may be at ADAH.
- **Spanish colonial militia:** Records of the Mobile district militia under Spanish rule are at the **Archivo General de Indias**, Seville, Spain, with copies at the Library of Congress.

### Cemetery Records
- **Online coverage:** Find A Grave is a curated external resource confirmed for this place and period. Coverage for pre-1820 graves is sparse but growing.
- **Physical records:** Many early burials were in family or church cemeteries with no sexton records. Catholic churchyard records in Mobile are among the best-documented early burial records.

### Newspaper Records
- **Local papers:** Newspaper publishing in the Alabama area was minimal before statehood. The *Mobile Centinel* (founded 1811) and the *Huntsville Republican* (founded 1816) are among the earliest. Newspapers.com is confirmed as a curated external resource.
- **Content value:** Obituaries, legal notices, and land sale advertisements in the territorial press can document individuals. Coverage for ca. 1800 specifically is very limited.

### Tax Records
- **Types:** Territorial-era property and poll tax lists
- **Date range:** Territorial tax lists begin ca. 1800–1810 for parts of the Alabama area
- **Where held:** Primarily at **ADAH** in Montgomery; some transcribed versions have been published by local genealogical societies. Tax lists serve as a critical **substitute for the missing 1800 federal census** in this area.
- **Substitute value:** Because there is no 1800 federal census for Alabama and no civil vital records, tax lists are often the only annual documentation of a male head of household in this period.

### Colonial Records (French, British, and Spanish)
This is a unique category for ca. 1800 research in Alabama, especially the Mobile area:

- **French colonial records (1702–1763):** Archives Nationales d'Outre-Mer (ANOM), Aix-en-Provence, France. Microfilm copies at the **Mobile Municipal Archives**. Records include census returns, land concessions, and church registers.
- **Spanish records, Mobile District (1780–1813):** Archivo General de Indias (AGI), Seville, Spain. The Library of Congress holds copies. Include census returns, church registers, and land grants. Records are in **Spanish**; some are in **French**.
- **British West Florida records (1763–1780):** British colonial archives (now at The National Archives, Kew, UK). Some copies held at the Library of Congress and the Mississippi Department of Archives and History (MDAH).

---

## Repository Guide

### Online Repositories
| Repository | Key Collections for This Place & Period | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** | Alabama, County Marriages 1809–1950 (coll. 1598408); Alabama, Probate Records 1809–1985 (coll. 1537764); Alabama, Compiled Census & Substitutes 1810–1890 (coll. 2016756); Alabama, Church Records 1803–1950 (coll. 1986396); **Mississippi**, Territorial & State Land Records 1798–1907 (coll. 1919694); **Mississippi**, County Court Records 1799–1920 (coll. 1855293); **Mississippi**, Compiled Census & Substitutes 1792–1890 (coll. 1927171); **Mississippi**, Church Records 1800–1950 (coll. 2034891) | Free |
| **BLM General Land Office** (glorecords.blm.gov) | Federal land patents from ca. 1803 onward | Free |
| **Ancestry.com** | U.S. Federal Census collection (1810 onward for Alabama area) | Subscription |
| **Fold3** | Military pension and service records | Subscription |
| **Find A Grave** | Cemetery records | Free |
| **NARA (archives.gov)** | Military pensions, federal land files | Free finding aids; records by request |
| **Newspapers.com** | Historical newspapers (limited pre-1820 Alabama coverage) | Subscription |

### Physical Repositories
| Repository | Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Alabama Dept. of Archives & History (ADAH)**, Montgomery, AL | Mississippi Territory censuses (1801, 1805, 1808, 1810); territorial-era tax lists; early county records; state records from 1819 | In-person; limited remote request |
| **National Archives**, Washington, D.C. | Military pension files; bounty land warrants; federal land entry files; territorial papers | In-person; microfilm; some digitized via Fold3/Ancestry |
| **National Archives, Atlanta (Southeast Region)** | Federal records relating to Alabama and Southeast | In-person |
| **Mobile Municipal Archives**, Mobile, AL | French colonial records (microfilm); local city records | In-person |
| **Library of Congress**, Washington, D.C. | Copies of Spanish AGI records; British colonial records; early newspapers | In-person; some digitized online |
| **Archivo General de Indias (AGI)**, Seville, Spain | Original Spanish colonial records for Mobile district | In-person; digital images via PARES portal |
| **Archives Nationales d'Outre-Mer (ANOM)**, Aix-en-Provence, France | French colonial records for Mobile (1702–1763) | In-person; some microfilm |
| **Mississippi Dept. of Archives & History (MDAH)**, Jackson, MS | Mississippi Territory records; some Alabama-area territorial materials | In-person; some online |

---

## Records NOT Online

The following exist but are not available in any online database as of this survey:

- **Mississippi Territory censuses of 1801, 1805, 1808** (the 1810 is partially indexed) — physical only at ADAH
- **Territorial-era tax lists** for the Alabama portion of the Mississippi Territory — physical or microfilm at ADAH
- **Spanish colonial church registers** for the Mobile area (some partial microfilm at Mobile Municipal Archives, originals at AGI Seville)
- **French colonial records** (microfilm at Mobile Municipal Archives; originals at ANOM France)
- **British West Florida records** — originals at The National Archives, Kew, UK
- **Early county deed and court records** (pre-1820) for the handful of counties formed before statehood — held at individual county courthouses or ADAH; largely not digitized

---

## Known Record Losses

- **General courthouse vulnerability:** Alabama courthouses were vulnerable to fire, flood, and Civil War damage. For the pre-statehood period specifically, the territorial government infrastructure was thin and record survival is uneven.
- **1800 federal census:** The Alabama area was not enumerated in the 1800 federal census. No equivalent substitute fully replaces it — tax lists and the 1801 territorial census are the closest alternatives.
- **Frontier Protestant church records:** Many early Methodist and Baptist congregations in the territory kept no formal registers, or registers have been lost. Substitute: denominational conference minutes, which sometimes record baptisms and memberships.

---

## Research Tips

1. **Always search Mississippi before Alabama** for pre-1817 records on FamilySearch. The territorial-era collections are filed under the Mississippi scope. A failure to find an ancestor in Alabama collections does not mean the record doesn't exist — check Mississippi.
2. **The Mobile area requires a completely separate research track.** Spanish colonial records (in Spanish) cover the Mobile district through 1813. Begin with the AGI catalog (PARES portal) and Library of Congress holdings before assuming no pre-1813 records exist for a Mobile-area ancestor.
3. **Land records are your anchor.** Because there are no civil vital records and the 1800 census doesn't cover this area, federal land patents and territorial land records are often the first solid documentation of a settler. Begin with BLM-GLO and collection 1919694 on FamilySearch.
4. **Tax lists substitute for the census.** Territorial-era tax lists at ADAH are a year-by-year presence record for taxable males. Request copies from ADAH or consult published transcriptions from Alabama genealogical societies.
5. **The 1810 census is your earliest federal enumeration.** Use FamilySearch collection 2016756 (Alabama census substitutes, starting 1810) and collection 1927171 (Mississippi compiled census, from 1792) together — they cross-index the territorial enumerations.
6. **Post-1820 pension files look back.** Military pension applications filed in the 1820s–1840s by veterans of the Creek War and War of 1812 typically include affidavits with retrospective biographical detail covering the 1800–1815 period. These are invaluable for placing a family in a location before county records existed.
7. **Work the FamilySearch Wiki's Alabama Genealogy article.** The wiki search returned the article at `https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Alabama_Genealogy` — its "Territorial Records" and "Colonial Records" sections give additional repository and collection guidance specific to this period.
8. **DAR and local genealogical society publications.** The Alabama Genealogical Society and county-level societies have published many transcriptions of territorial-era records. Check ADAH's published holdings before traveling.

---

# Locality Guide: Alabama Region, Pre-Statehood (circa 1780–1819)

## ⚠️ The Most Important Thing to Know Up Front

The Alabama region around 1800 passed through **multiple governing jurisdictions**, and records are split accordingly. There is **no single "Alabama" record set** for this period — you must work at least two separate research tracks:

| Period | Jurisdiction | Where records are held |
|---|---|---|
| 1702–1763 | French Colony (Mobile area) | ANOM, Aix-en-Provence; Mobile Municipal Archives (microfilm) |
| 1763–1780 | British West Florida | The National Archives, Kew, UK; Library of Congress copies |
| 1780–1813 | **Spanish West Florida, Mobile District** | Archivo General de Indias, Seville; Library of Congress copies |
| 1798–1817 | **Mississippi Territory** (inland areas) | FamilySearch (under **Mississippi**, not Alabama); ADAH, Montgomery |
| 1817–1819 | Alabama Territory | FamilySearch Alabama collections; ADAH |
| 1819– | State of Alabama | FamilySearch Alabama collections; ADAH |

---

## Jurisdiction Overview

- **Parent at ca. 1800:** The Mississippi Territory (est. 7 April 1798) governed inland Alabama. The Mobile district was still under **Spanish colonial administration** until April 1813, when U.S. forces took Fort Charlotte.
- **Economy:** Subsistence farming, frontier cotton cultivation, fur trade, riverine commerce
- **Settlers:** Anglo-American and Scots-Irish settlers arriving along river corridors from the Carolinas, Virginia, and Georgia; French/Spanish Creole communities in Mobile; Creek and Cherokee nations across the interior; enslaved African Americans throughout
- **Denominations:** Roman Catholic (Mobile, French/Spanish heritage); Methodist and Baptist frontier churches; Presbyterian missions to Native peoples
- **Key events:** U.S. takeover of Mobile (1813); Creek War/Battle of Horseshoe Bend (1813–1814); Alabama Territory formed (1817); statehood (1819)

---

## Available Record Types

### Vital Records (Civil Registration)
- **Critical gap:** Alabama did **not** begin statewide civil birth/death registration until **1908**. For ca. 1800, there are **no government-issued birth or death certificates**. None.
- **Marriages:** County marriage records begin only at county formation — few Alabama counties existed in 1800. **FamilySearch collection 1598408** — *Alabama, County Marriages, 1809–1950* (612,345 records, 306,172 images) — is the earliest indexed series. **Indexed + images.**
- **Substitute:** Church registers are the only vital-event source for the ca. 1800 period.

### Church Records
- **Catholic, Mobile area:** Sacramental registers (baptisms, marriages, burials) survive from the French and Spanish eras. Records are in **Latin, French, and Spanish** — the researcher must read the original languages. Held at the **Mobile Catholic Archdiocese Archives**; microfilm at the Mobile Municipal Archives.
- **Protestant frontier:** **FamilySearch collection 1986396** — *Alabama, Church Records, 1803–1950* (156,789 records, 78,394 images). **FamilySearch collection 2034891** — *Mississippi, Church Records, 1800–1950* (87,654 records, 43,827 images) — may cover congregations that straddled the territorial boundary.
- Early frontier Baptist and Methodist congregations often kept **no formal registers**; denominational conference minutes are a substitute.

### Census Records
- **The 1800 federal census does not cover the Alabama area.** The earliest federal enumeration is **1810**.
- **1810 census:** Indexed in **FamilySearch collection 2016756** — *Alabama, Compiled Census and Census Substitutes Index, 1810–1890* (287,634 index entries, **no images on FamilySearch**) and in **collection 1927171** — *Mississippi, Compiled Census and Census Substitutes, 1792–1890* (198,340 records). Use both together.
- **Territorial censuses 1801, 1805, 1808:** Per the FamilySearch Wiki *Alabama Genealogy* article, these are held **physically at ADAH in Montgomery** — not online. These are the closest thing to a 1800-era "census" for the inland Alabama area.

### Probate and Court Records
- **FamilySearch collection 1537764** — *Alabama, Probate Records, 1809–1985* (438,210 records, 219,105 images) — begins at county formation, ca. 1809. **Indexed + images.**
- **FamilySearch collection 1855293** — *Mississippi, County Court Records, 1799–1920* (245,670 records, 122,835 images) — covers the Mississippi Territory court system that governed Alabama's interior before 1817. **Search this for pre-statehood court filings.**

### Land Records — Your Most Important Source
Because there are no civil vital records and the 1800 census doesn't cover this area, **federal land patents are often the first solid documentation of a settler**.

- **System:** Federal-land state — land distributed by the U.S. General Land Office via cash sale, warrant, and patent after survey. The **St. Stephens Land Office** (opened 1803) and the **Huntsville Land Office** (opened 1809) were the earliest in present-day Alabama.
- **BLM General Land Office Records** (glorecords.blm.gov) — free, searchable online
- **FamilySearch collection 1919694** — *Mississippi, Territorial and State Land Records, 1798–1907* (312,450 records, 156,225 images). **Indexed + images.** This is the FamilySearch home for territorial-era Alabama land records.
- **Spanish land grants (Mobile district):** Library of Congress holds copies; originals at Archivo General de Indias, Seville.

### Military Records
- **Conflicts:** Creek War (1813–1814), War of 1812; earlier territorial militia service
- **Post-1820 pension files look backward:** Pension applications filed in the 1820s–1840s by Creek War and War of 1812 veterans routinely include retrospective affidavits covering the 1800–1815 period — invaluable for placing a family before county records existed.
- **Where held:** NARA, Washington D.C. (pension/bounty land files). **Fold3** (confirmed external link) indexes many. State militia records at ADAH.
- **Spanish colonial militia (Mobile):** Records at Archivo General de Indias, Seville; copies at Library of Congress. In Spanish.

### Tax Records — Primary Census Substitute
- Territorial-era property and poll tax lists exist for the Alabama area from ca. 1800–1810 onward.
- Held at **ADAH, Montgomery** — physical or microfilm only; **not available online**. Some transcriptions published by Alabama genealogical societies.
- **These are the primary substitute for the missing 1800 federal census** — they provide a year-by-year presence record for taxable males.

### Colonial Records (Unique to This Period)
- **French (1702–1763):** Archives Nationales d'Outre-Mer (ANOM), Aix-en-Provence, France. Microfilm copies at Mobile Municipal Archives. Include census returns, land concessions, church registers. In French.
- **Spanish (1780–1813):** Archivo General de Indias (AGI), Seville, Spain (PARES portal has some digital images). Library of Congress holds copies. Include census returns, church registers, land grants. In Spanish.
- **British (1763–1780):** The National Archives, Kew, UK. Library of Congress and Mississippi Dept. of Archives & History (MDAH) hold some copies.

### Cemetery & Newspaper Records
- **Find A Grave** (confirmed curated resource): pre-1820 grave coverage is sparse but growing.
- **Newspapers.com** (confirmed curated resource): Newspaper publishing in the Alabama area was minimal before statehood; the *Mobile Centinel* (founded 1811) and *Huntsville Republican* (founded 1816) are among the earliest. Very limited pre-1820 coverage.

---

## Repository Guide

### Online
| Repository | Key Collections | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** | Colls. 1598408, 1537764, 2016756, 1986396 (Alabama); 1919694, 1855293, 1927171, 2034891 (**Mississippi**) | Free |
| **BLM-GLO** (glorecords.blm.gov) | Federal land patents from 1803 | Free |
| **Ancestry.com** | U.S. Federal Census (1810+) | Subscription |
| **Fold3** | Military pension and service records | Subscription |
| **Find A Grave** | Cemetery records | Free |
| **NARA** (archives.gov) | Military pensions; land entry files | Free finding aids; records by request |

### Physical
| Repository | Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **ADAH**, Montgomery, AL | Territorial censuses (1801, 1805, 1808, 1810); tax lists; early county records | In-person / limited remote request |
| **NARA**, Washington, D.C. | Military pensions; bounty land warrants; territorial papers | In-person; some via Fold3/Ancestry |
| **Mobile Municipal Archives**, Mobile, AL | French colonial microfilm; local city records | In-person |
| **Library of Congress**, Washington, D.C. | Copies of Spanish/British colonial records; early newspapers | In-person; some digitized |
| **Archivo General de Indias (AGI)**, Seville, Spain | Spanish colonial originals for Mobile district | In-person; PARES portal (digital images) |
| **ANOM**, Aix-en-Provence, France | French colonial originals | In-person; some microfilm |
| **MDAH**, Jackson, MS | Mississippi Territory records; some Alabama-area materials | In-person; some online |

---

## Records NOT Online
- Mississippi Territory censuses of **1801, 1805, 1808** — ADAH only
- Territorial-era **tax lists** — ADAH; some published genealogical society transcriptions
- **Spanish colonial church registers**, Mobile area — originals at AGI Seville; some microfilm at Mobile Municipal Archives
- **French colonial records** — originals at ANOM; microfilm at Mobile Municipal Archives
- **British West Florida records** — TNA Kew; some copies at Library of Congress
- Most pre-1820 **county deed and court records** — individual county courthouses or ADAH

---

## Research Tips
1. **Always search Mississippi before Alabama on FamilySearch for pre-1817 records.** The territorial-era collections are scoped under Mississippi. Not finding an ancestor in Alabama collections is not a negative result until you've checked Mississippi too.
2. **Mobile = a completely separate research track.** Spanish colonial records (in Spanish) cover the Mobile district through 1813. Begin with the AGI/PARES portal and Library of Congress before assuming no pre-1813 records exist for a Mobile-area ancestor.
3. **Land records are your anchor.** Start with BLM-GLO and FamilySearch collection 1919694 — these are often the first federal documentation of a settler.
4. **Tax lists substitute for the 1800 census.** Contact ADAH or look for published transcriptions from Alabama genealogical societies.
5. **Pension files look backward.** Creek War and War of 1812 pension applications from the 1820s–1840s contain retrospective affidavits covering the pre-county period.
6. **Consult the FamilySearch Wiki article "Alabama Genealogy"** at `https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Alabama_Genealogy` — its "Territorial Records" and "Colonial Records" sections give additional repository detail specific to this period.

Military pension applications filed in the 1820s-1840s by Creek War and War of 1812 veterans often contain retrospective biographical affidavits covering the 1800-1815 period.
entry.guide_markdown:
# Locality Guide: Alabama Region, Pre-Statehood (circa 1780–1819)

## Jurisdiction Overview

- **Formed:** Alabama Territory created 1817 from the eastern portion of the Mississippi Territory; achieved statehood 3 December 1819 as the 27th state
- **Administrative centers:** Washington (territorial capital, 1817–1819); St. Stephens was the major settlement in the Alabama area ca. 1800
- **Parent jurisdiction at ca. 1800:** Mississippi Territory (established 7 April 1798); the Mobile district specifically remained under **Spanish colonial administration** until U.S. forces took Fort Charlotte in April 1813
- **Economy:** Subsistence farming, cotton cultivation beginning in the early 1800s, fur trade, riverine commerce
- **Ethnic composition:** Creek and Cherokee nations occupied much of the interior; European settlers (Anglo-American, Scots-Irish) settling along rivers; French and Spanish Creole communities in Mobile; enslaved African Americans present throughout
- **Religious denominations:** Roman Catholic (Mobile area); Methodist and Baptist frontier congregations; Presbyterian missions
- **Key historical events:** Formation of Mississippi Territory (1798); U.S. acquisition of Mobile from Spain (1813); Creek War / Battle of Horseshoe Bend (1813–1814); Alabama Territory (1817); statehood (1819)

## Boundary Changes — Critical for Research

| Period | Jurisdiction |
|---|---|
| 1702–1763 | French Colony (Mobile area) |
| 1763–1780 | British West Florida |
| 1780–1813 | Spanish West Florida (Mobile district) |
| 1798–1817 | Mississippi Territory (inland areas) |
| 1817–1819 | Alabama Territory |
| 1819– | State of Alabama |

⚠️ Records for the pre-1817 inland Alabama area are filed under **Mississippi** (not Alabama) in FamilySearch.

## Available Record Types

### Vital Records
- **Civil registration:** Did not begin until 1908. No civil birth/death certificates exist for ca. 1800.
- **Marriages:** FamilySearch coll. 1598408 (Alabama, County Marriages 1809–1950; 612,345 records, 306,172 images) is the earliest indexed series.
- **Substitute:** Church registers.

### Church Records
- Catholic Mobile-area registers: French and Spanish periods, in French/Spanish/Latin. Mobile Catholic Archdiocese Archives.
- FamilySearch coll. 1986396 (Alabama, Church Records 1803–1950; 156,789 records, 78,394 images)
- FamilySearch coll. 2034891 (Mississippi, Church Records 1800–1950; 87,654 records, 43,827 images)

### Census Records
- 1800 federal census: **does not cover Alabama area**
- 1810 federal census: first enumeration — FamilySearch coll. 2016756 (287,634 index entries) and coll. 1927171 (198,340 records)
- Territorial censuses 1801, 1805, 1808: physical only at ADAH, Montgomery

### Probate and Court Records
- FamilySearch coll. 1537764 (Alabama, Probate Records 1809–1985; 438,210 records, 219,105 images)
- FamilySearch coll. 1855293 (Mississippi, County Court Records 1799–1920; 245,670 records, 122,835 images) — covers territorial courts

### Land Records
- Federal land patents: BLM-GLO (glorecords.blm.gov) — free, searchable
- FamilySearch coll. 1919694 (Mississippi, Territorial and State Land Records 1798–1907; 312,450 records, 156,225 images)
- Spanish land grants (Mobile): Library of Congress copies; originals at AGI Seville

### Military Records
- Creek War (1813–1814), War of 1812 pension files at NARA; Fold3 indexes many
- Spanish colonial militia: Archivo General de Indias, Seville; copies at Library of Congress

### Tax Records
- Territorial-era tax lists at ADAH — primary substitute for missing 1800 census
- Not available online; in-person visit or records request required

### Colonial Records (French, British, Spanish)
- French (1702–1763): ANOM, Aix-en-Provence; microfilm at Mobile Municipal Archives
- Spanish (1780–1813): AGI Seville; Library of Congress copies; records in Spanish
- British (1763–1780): The National Archives, Kew, UK; Library of Congress copies

## Repository Guide

| Repository | Key Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| FamilySearch | 8 overlapping collections (see above) | Free |
| BLM-GLO (glorecords.blm.gov) | Federal land patents from 1803 | Free |
| Ancestry.com | U.S. Federal Census (1810+) | Subscription |
| Fold3 | Military pensions/service records | Subscription |
| ADAH, Montgomery AL | Territorial censuses; tax lists; early county records | In-person/request |
| NARA, Washington D.C. | Military pensions; land entry files; territorial papers | In-person/microfilm |
| Mobile Municipal Archives | French colonial microfilm; local records | In-person |
| Library of Congress | Spanish/British colonial copies; early newspapers | In-person/some online |
| AGI, Seville, Spain | Spanish colonial originals | In-person/PARES portal |
| ANOM, Aix-en-Provence, France | French colonial originals | In-person/some microfilm |
| MDAH, Jackson MS | Mississippi Territory records | In-person/some online |

## Records NOT Online
- Mississippi Territory censuses 1801, 1805, 1808 (ADAH only)
- Territorial-era tax lists (ADAH; some published transcriptions)
- Spanish colonial church registers for Mobile (AGI/Mobile Municipal Archives)
- French colonial records (ANOM/Mobile Municipal Archives)
- British West Florida records (TNA Kew/Library of Congress)
- Most pre-1820 county deed and court records

## Research Tips
1. Always search Mississippi before Alabama on FamilySearch for pre-1817 records.
2. Mobile area requires a separate Spanish-colonial research track (Spanish-language records).
3. Land records are the anchor — begin with BLM-GLO and FamilySearch coll. 1919694.
4. Tax lists at ADAH substitute for the missing 1800 census.
5. Post-1820 pension files contain retrospective biographical detail covering the 1800–1815 period.
6. Consult the FamilySearch Wiki article 'Alabama Genealogy' (https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Alabama_Genealogy) for additional territorial and colonial record guidance.

quirks: Pre-1817 Alabama records on FamilySearch are filed under Mississippi (not Alabama) — search Mississippi-scoped collections for any ancestor in the territory before 1817.
Alabama had no statewide civil registration of births or deaths until 1908 — no civil vital records exist for the ca. 1800 period.
The 1800 federal census did not cover the Alabama area; the earliest federal enumeration is the 1810 census (Mississippi Territory).
Mississippi Territory censuses of 1801, 1805, and 1808 are held physically at the Alabama Department of Archives and History (ADAH) in Montgomery — not available online.

The Mobile district was under Spanish colonial administration until April 1813; Mobile-area records are in Spanish (and some French) at the Archivo General de Indias, Seville, with copies at the Library of Congress.

Federal land patents from the St. Stephens Land Office (opened 1803) and Huntsville Land Office (opened 1809) are the earliest federal documentation of most Alabama-area settlers — search BLM-GLO (glorecords.blm.gov) first.
Territorial-era tax lists at ADAH serve as the primary census substitute for the missing 1800 enumeration.
Military pension applications filed in the 1820s-1840s by Creek War and War of 1812 veterans often contain retrospective biographical affidavits covering the 1800-1815 period.

output.text_response:
Now I'll fire all the survey calls in one parallel batch.Now let me read the Arkansas Genealogy wiki page for more detail.I now have all the survey data I need. Here is the full locality research guide.

---

## 📋 Locality Research Guide
### Arkansas Region · Pre-1800 (Spanish & French Colonial Era)

---

### Jurisdictional Context

The land that is now Arkansas did not exist as a political entity before 1800. It passed through three colonial administrations:

| Period | Jurisdiction | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| 1686–1763 | **French Louisiana** ("Poste des Arkansas") | Arkansas Post founded 1686 as first European settlement in lower Mississippi Valley |
| 1763–1800 | **Spanish Louisiana** ("Distrito de Arkansas") | Ceded by France via Treaty of Paris (1763); Spain returned it secretly in 1800 |
| 1800–1803 | **French Louisiana** (briefly) | Ceded back to France, then sold in the Louisiana Purchase |
| 1803–1819 | **U.S. Territory of Louisiana / Missouri Territory** | No organized Arkansas jurisdiction yet |
| 1819– | **Arkansas Territory** | First organized Arkansas jurisdiction |
| 1836– | **State of Arkansas** | Statehood |

**Research implication:** Records created before 1800 were generated under French and Spanish colonial administrations, not by any Arkansas government. Settlement was almost entirely concentrated at **Arkansas Post** (present-day Arkansas County). Any ancestor living in the region during this period was almost certainly recorded at or through that post.

---

### Record Types, Availability & Repositories

#### 1. 🏛️ Sacramental Registers (Catholic Church)
**Coverage:** Baptisms, marriages, burials — 1700s through 1800

- **FamilySearch digitized volume:** Image Group **008814402** — 64 images, covering **Arkansas Post, 1763–1800**, record type: *Sacramental Registers (Spanish Colonial)*
  - ⚠️ **Browse-only — 0% name-indexed, not full-text searchable.** Must be browsed image-by-image.
  - ⚠️ **Written in Spanish.** Researcher must be able to read Spanish-language Catholic registers.
- French Catholic missionaries maintained registers from the early 1700s onward; some French-language entries predate the Spanish period.
- Physical/microfilm copies also held at:
  - **Roman Catholic Diocese of Little Rock** (Little Rock, AR)
  - **Arkansas History Commission** (Little Rock, AR) — transcribed copies available

#### 2. 📊 Spanish Colonial Censuses (Padrones)
**Coverage:** Periodic household censuses of the Arkansas Post settlement, 1763–1800

- Spanish administrators conducted *padrones* naming heads of household and family members.
- **No FamilySearch indexed collection or digitized volume** was found for this record type within the target period.
- Surviving padrones are held at:
  - **Arkansas History Commission**, Little Rock, AR
  - **Archivo General de Indias**, Seville, Spain (primary Spanish colonial archive)
  - **Library of Congress** — holds microfilm of many Spanish colonial Louisiana records

#### 3. 🌾 Land Grants & Concessions
**Coverage:** Spanish and French land grants, 1700s–1800

- Spain issued land concessions to settlers at Arkansas Post. After the Louisiana Purchase (1803), the U.S. Land Office adjudicated these grants — confirming or rejecting them — generating a second set of records.
- Held at:
  - **Arkansas History Commission** — original Spanish grant documents
  - **National Archives and Records Administration (NARA)** — records of the U.S. Land Office adjudication of French and Spanish claims (post-1803); searchable via [archives.gov](https://www.archives.gov/)
  - **Bureau of Land Management General Land Office (BLM GLO)** — confirmed patent records post-1803; free search at [glorecords.blm.gov](https://glorecords.blm.gov)
- **FamilySearch "Arkansas, Compiled Census and Census Substitutes Index, 1819–1870"** (Collection [2085124](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2085124)) begins in 1819 and may include early territorial substitutes that reference pre-1800 land claimants.

#### 4. ⚔️ Military Records
**Coverage:** Spanish garrison records, Arkansas Post, 1763–1800

- Arkansas Post was an active Spanish military garrison. Muster rolls, payrolls, and personnel records were generated.
- **No FamilySearch digitized volume** found for Spanish military records of this post.
- Held at:
  - **Archivo General de Indias**, Seville, Spain
  - **Archivo General de Simancas**, Valladolid, Spain (Spanish military records)
  - **[Fold3.com](https://www.fold3.com/)** — check for any transcribed/digitized Spanish colonial military records

#### 5. 🏛️ Civil & Administrative Records
**Coverage:** Spanish colonial government documents, 1763–1800

- The Spanish commandant at Arkansas Post generated civil records: correspondence, judicial proceedings, trade permits, and administrative registers.
- **No FamilySearch indexed collection or digitized volume** was returned for this record type in the target period.
- Held at:
  - **Arkansas History Commission**, Little Rock, AR — primary in-state repository for surviving colonial records
  - **Archivo General de Indias**, Seville, Spain — the ultimate repository for Spanish colonial Louisiana governance

---

### Repository Summary

| Repository | What They Hold | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Arkansas History Commission**, Little Rock, AR | Surviving colonial padrones, land records, church transcriptions, administrative records | In-person; some finding aids online at [archives.arkansas.gov](https://www.archives.arkansas.gov) |
| **Roman Catholic Diocese of Little Rock** | Original/transcribed Catholic sacramental registers | Contact the Diocese directly |
| **FamilySearch** (Image Group 008814402) | 64-image browse-only digitized sacramental register, 1763–1800, Spanish | Free at [familysearch.org](https://www.familysearch.org) — must browse image-by-image |
| **Archivo General de Indias**, Seville, Spain | Spanish colonial censuses, military records, land grants, civil administration | In-person or via [pares.mcu.es](https://pares.mcu.es) (Portal de Archivos Españoles) |
| **Library of Congress**, Washington, D.C. | Microfilm of Spanish colonial Louisiana records | In-person or via Manuscript Division |
| **NARA** | U.S. Land Office adjudications of French/Spanish land claims (post-1803) | In-person or [archives.gov](https://www.archives.gov) |
| **BLM GLO** | Federal land patents (post-1803, confirmed grants) | Free at [glorecords.blm.gov](https://glorecords.blm.gov) |

---

### Digitization Summary

| Record Type | FamilySearch Status | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Sacramental registers (1763–1800) | ⚠️ Browse-only images (64 images) | Spanish language; 0% indexed; Image Group 008814402 |
| Colonial censuses (padrones) | ❌ Not digitized on FamilySearch | Consult Arkansas History Commission & Archivo General de Indias |
| Spanish land grants | ❌ Not digitized for colonial period | Post-1803 U.S. adjudication records at NARA/BLM GLO |
| Military records | ❌ Not digitized on FamilySearch | Spanish national archives in Seville and Valladolid |
| Civil/administrative records | ❌ Not digitized on FamilySearch | Arkansas History Commission; Archivo General de Indias |

---

### ⚠️ Critical Research Quirks

1. **Population was tiny and concentrated.** Virtually all European settlers in the Arkansas region before 1800 lived at or near **Arkansas Post**. If your ancestor was in "Arkansas," they were almost certainly at that one location.

2. **No Arkansas civil registration existed before 1836.** State vital records don't begin until statehood. For this period, Catholic sacramental registers are the only vital-event source.

3. **Spanish records are in Spanish; earlier French records are in French.** The only FamilySearch digitized volume (Image Group 008814402) is entirely in Spanish and must be read in the original language — no transcription or index exists.

4. **Follow the land claim trail through 1803.** If your ancestor held a Spanish land grant, they had to petition the U.S. Land Office after the Louisiana Purchase. Those post-1803 U.S. records often name, describe, and document the original Spanish-era claimant in detail — making them an essential bridge source even for pre-1800 research.

5. **Spanish archives in Spain are essential.** The Archivo General de Indias in Seville is the primary repository for Spanish colonial Louisiana governance and cannot be bypassed for serious pre-1800 Arkansas research. The Portal de Archivos Españoles ([pares.mcu.es](https://pares.mcu.es)) provides some online access.

6. **FamilySearch collections for Arkansas start in 1819.** The three indexed FamilySearch collections (marriages 1837–1957; Freedmen's Bureau 1865–1872; census substitutes 1819–1870) are all post-period for pre-1800 research, though the census substitutes index may include references to early territorial settlers whose origins trace to the colonial era.

---

*Sources: FamilySearch Wiki "Arkansas Genealogy" (Colonial Records, Church Records sections); FamilySearch volume_search (Image Group 008814402); Wikipedia "Arkansas"; collections_search results for Arkansas, United States.*

# 🗺️ Locality Research Guide: Ohio — Pre-Statehood Period (~1783–1803)

**Jurisdiction surveyed:** Ohio, United States (`standardPlace`: `Ohio, United States`)
**Target window:** c. 1783–1803 (focused on 1790)
**Note:** In 1790, "Ohio" did not exist as a legal jurisdiction. Your ancestors lived in the **Northwest Territory**, the federal territory established by the Northwest Ordinance of 1787. FamilySearch's `Ohio, United States` place record begins at statehood (1803). Records created before that date were made under territorial or county authority — not the State of Ohio.

---

## 1. Jurisdictional History & Boundary Changes

This is the single most important thing to understand before searching any record.

| Date | Event | Research Impact |
|------|-------|-----------------|
| 1787 | **Northwest Ordinance** creates the Northwest Territory, encompassing present-day Ohio, Indiana, Illinois, Michigan, Wisconsin, and part of Minnesota | All records 1787–1803 filed under territorial authority |
| **1788** | **Washington County** organized — the very first county in what would become Ohio, centered on Marietta | The only county authority for most of Ohio in 1790 |
| **1790** | **Hamilton County** organized, in the southwest (Cincinnati area) | Splits jurisdiction: settlers in southwest now file under Hamilton; elsewhere still Washington |
| 1790s–1800s | Rapid county subdivision continues (St. Clair 1798, Adams 1797, etc.) | You must determine which county held jurisdiction at the exact date of your event |
| 1799 | Northwest Territory's eastern portion (present Ohio) gets its own territorial legislature | Increased local record-keeping begins |
| **1803** | **Ohio admitted as the 17th state** — first state carved from the Northwest Territory | State-level records begin; county records continue unbroken |

**Critical rule for 1790 research:** Before searching any collection, identify whether your ancestor lived in the Washington County jurisdiction (most of Ohio) or the Hamilton County jurisdiction (southwest). Records will be filed under whichever county held authority at the time — not under "Ohio."

### Two Distinct Land Districts (from the FamilySearch Wiki, "Ohio Genealogy — Land Records")

Ohio has **two internal land systems** that create separate record trails:

- **Virginia Military District** (between the Scioto and Little Miami rivers, south-central Ohio): Land was granted to Virginia's Revolutionary War veterans using a **metes-and-bounds survey** — irregular lot shapes, no standard grid. Warrants for this district are held at the **Virginia State Library**.
- **Connecticut Western Reserve** (northeastern Ohio, roughly modern Cuyahoga, Geauga, Lake, and neighboring counties): Surveyed on a distinct township grid. Records are held at the **Western Reserve Historical Society**, Cleveland.
- **Remainder of Ohio** (outside those two districts): Federal lands surveyed under the standard rectangular Public Land Survey System; patents at the **BLM General Land Office** (glorecords.blm.gov).

If you don't know which district your ancestor was in, establishing that first will determine where to look for land records.

---

## 2. Available Records & Access Levels

### 📄 Land Records — **PRIMARY SOURCE for 1790**

Land records are the **most important substitute** for nearly all missing record types in this period. Even without vital records or a census, land patents, warrants, and deeds place a person at a specific location at a specific time.

**FamilySearch collection:**
- **Ohio, Land Records, 1787–1840** (Collection ID: 1542187) — 234,567 records, 117,283 images
  - Date range begins 1787, directly covering the 1790 target window
  - Fully overlaps the territorial period
  - [Search on FamilySearch](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1542187)

**FamilySearch digitized volume (from volume_search):**
- **Northwest Territory Territorial Land Records, 1788–1799** (Image Group: 005112233)
  - 51 images; **0% name-indexed; not full-text searchable**
  - ⚠️ **Browse-only** — must be examined image by image with no search capability
  - Filed under "Northwest Territory, United States" — search using that place name, not "Ohio"
  - This volume and the Ohio, Land Records collection represent **one continuous research trail** across the territorial/statehood boundary — work both together

**Other repositories:**
- Virginia Military District warrants → **Virginia State Library**, Richmond, VA
- Connecticut Western Reserve grants → **Western Reserve Historical Society**, Cleveland, OH
- Federal land patents → **BLM General Land Office** ([glorecords.blm.gov](https://glorecords.blm.gov))
- Original territorial and early county deed books → **Ohio History Connection**, Columbus, OH

---

### 💍 Marriage Records — **LIMITED COVERAGE**

**FamilySearch collection:**
- **Ohio, County Marriages, 1789–2013** (Collection ID: 1803785) — 1,523,678 records, 761,839 images
  - Begins 1789, so the very earliest years of the target window are potentially covered
  - [Search on FamilySearch](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1803785)
  - ⚠️ Marriage recording in 1790 Ohio was inconsistent — official licenses were not universally required in the territorial period. Expect gaps; treat absence of a record as inconclusive.

---

### ⛪ Church Records — **OFTEN THE ONLY VITAL SOURCE FOR 1790**

Civil registration of births and deaths did not exist in 1790 Ohio. Church registers are typically the **only documentary source** for births, baptisms, and burials in this period.

**FamilySearch collection:**
- **Ohio, Church Records, 1787–1950** (Collection ID: 2246917) — 678,234 records, 339,117 images
  - Begins 1787, covering the full target window
  - Includes baptisms, marriages, and burials from multiple denominations
  - [Search on FamilySearch](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2246917)

**Coverage is uneven.** Settlement in 1790 Ohio was sparse and concentrated near Marietta (Washington County) and Cincinnati (Hamilton County). Many settlers were unchurched or belonged to congregations that did not yet maintain formal registers. Physical-only records may survive at:
- Denominational archives (Methodist, Presbyterian, Baptist, Quaker)
- Ohio History Connection, Columbus

---

### 💰 Tax Records — **BEST ANNUAL LOCATOR AFTER LAND RECORDS**

Tax lists are taken annually and can pinpoint a person in a county year by year — especially valuable when other records are missing.

**FamilySearch collection:**
- **Ohio, Tax Records, 1800–1850** (Collection ID: 1987542) — 345,890 records, 172,945 images
  - ⚠️ Begins **1800**, so it does **not** cover 1790
  - Useful for the statehood period immediately following, and to find where an ancestor settled after 1800
  - [Search on FamilySearch](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1987542)

**FamilySearch digitized volume (from volume_search):**
- **Washington County, Ohio, County Tax Records, 1799–1803** (Image Group: 005112240)
  - 73 images; **12% name-indexed**; not full-text searchable
  - Partially searchable by name; remainder must be browsed
  - Bridges the territorial-to-statehood transition — work this volume alongside the tax collection above as one continuous trail
  - Filed under Washington County, Ohio — the county that held authority over most of Ohio before further subdivision

**Gap for 1790:** No digitized or indexed Ohio/Northwest Territory tax records for the period before 1799 were returned by the volume search. Territorial-era tax lists for 1788–1799, if they survive, are likely physical-only at the Ohio History Connection or county courthouses.

---

### 📊 Census Records

- **1790 Federal Census:** Ohio/Northwest Territory residents were generally **not enumerated** in the 1790 federal census — the census covered the existing states, not the territories. Researchers should not expect to find Ohio ancestors in the 1790 census.
- **1800 Federal Census:** The first federal census to include Ohio residents. Ohio statehood in 1803 means 1800 census records exist for the territorial population.
- State and territorial censuses for this period are not confirmed by the tools as digitized collections; check the Ohio History Connection for any surviving territorial enumeration fragments.

---

### ⚔️ Military Records

**FamilySearch external link:**
- **Fold3 — Military Records** (curated by FamilySearch for Ohio): [fold3.com](https://www.fold3.com)

**National Archives and Records Administration (NARA)** holds:
- Revolutionary War service records and pension files (many Ohio settlers were veterans)
- Bounty land warrants (often the mechanism by which veterans obtained Ohio land)
- Indian Wars records for the Northwest Territory period (e.g., Battle of Fallen Timbers, 1794; Treaty of Greenville, 1795)

Pension and bounty land warrant files frequently contain ages, birthplaces, family details, and migration history — often the richest biographical sources for men alive in 1790.

---

### ☠️ Death Records

**FamilySearch collection:**
- **Ohio, County Death Records, 1840–2001** (Collection ID: 1417891) — begins 1840
  - ⚠️ **Does not cover the 1790 target period** — listed here only as background context
  - No civil death registration existed in Ohio in 1790

For deaths in 1790, church burial registers (above) and cemetery inscriptions are the only likely sources.

**FamilySearch external link:**
- **Find A Grave — Cemetery Records**: [findagrave.com](https://www.findagrave.com)
  - Gravestone inscriptions may survive for early settlers even when no civil or church record does

---

### 📰 Newspapers

**FamilySearch external link:**
- **Newspapers.com — Historical Newspapers**: [newspapers.com](https://www.newspapers.com)
- Ohio's first newspaper, the *Centinel of the North-Western Territory*, began publication in Cincinnati in **November 1793**. Coverage before that date does not exist. After 1793, death notices, legal notices, and land sale announcements begin appearing.

---

## 3. Key Repositories

| Repository | What's Held | Access |
|------------|-------------|--------|
| **Ohio History Connection**, Columbus, OH | Territorial and early state records, county deed books, probate files, early tax lists, military records | In-person; some digitized |
| **National Archives and Records Administration (NARA)** | Federal land patents, military pension & bounty warrant files, federal census | [archives.gov](https://www.archives.gov); some online via Fold3/Ancestry |
| **BLM General Land Office** | Federal land patents for the Public Land Survey area | Free online: glorecords.blm.gov |
| **Virginia State Library**, Richmond, VA | Virginia Military District warrants | In-person; some digitized |
| **Western Reserve Historical Society**, Cleveland, OH | Connecticut Western Reserve land records | In-person |
| **County Courthouses** | Deed books, probate, early court records | In-person; some on FamilySearch microfilm |
| **FamilySearch** (online) | 5 indexed collections, 2 digitized volumes for target period | Free at familysearch.org |
| **Ancestry.com** | U.S. Federal Census and additional Ohio collections | Subscription |

---

## 4. Research Strategy for 1790 Ohio

Given the gaps in early documentation, work this sequence:

1. **Identify the county** that had jurisdiction in 1790 for your ancestor's location (almost certainly Washington County or Hamilton County).
2. **Search land records first** — the Ohio, Land Records 1787–1840 collection and the browse-only Northwest Territory Territorial Land Records volume (005112233). These are the most likely surviving sources for 1790.
3. **Search church records** (Ohio, Church Records 1787–1950) for any vital events — baptism is your best proxy for birth.
4. **Check military pension files at NARA** — many Ohio settlers of this era were Revolutionary War veterans, and pension files are genealogically rich.
5. **Bridge to post-1800 records**: The Washington County Tax Records volume (005112240, 1799–1803, 12% indexed) and the Ohio, Tax Records 1800–1850 collection pick up where territorial records leave off. Working backward from 1800–1810 evidence into the 1790 gap is often more productive than searching the gap directly.
6. **Newspapers** become available after November 1793 for the Cincinnati area; check Newspapers.com for the *Centinel of the North-Western Territory*.
7. **Virginia Military District or Western Reserve settlers** require consulting Virginia State Library or Western Reserve Historical Society in addition to the above.

---

## 5. Coverage Gaps Summary

| Gap | Detail |
|-----|--------|
| No population data | FamilySearch has no population figures for Ohio in this fixture |
| No 1790 federal census coverage | Northwest Territory was not enumerated in 1790 |
| No civil vital registration | Births and deaths not officially recorded; church records are the only substitute |
| Territorial tax records pre-1799 | Not found in digitized/indexed form; likely physical-only at Ohio History Connection |
| Two browse-only volumes | Territorial Land Records (005112233) and partial Washington County Tax Records (005112240, 12% indexed) require image-by-image browsing |
| Ohio-specific FamilySearch Wiki pages | The wiki place pages for Ohio did not return Ohio-specific content in this survey; consult the FamilySearch Wiki directly at `familysearch.org/en/wiki/Ohio_Genealogy` |

---

*Sources: FamilySearch collections_search (5 collections, scope: Ohio); volume_search (2 volumes); external_links_search (6 curated links, totalForPlace: 6); Wikipedia (Ohio article); FamilySearch Wiki search results ("Ohio Genealogy — Northwest Territory and Boundary Changes"; "Ohio Genealogy — Land Records").*
