# 🏅 Locality Research Guide — California, 1848–1860 (Gold Rush Era)

## Jurisdictional Context

| Milestone | Date |
|---|---|
| Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo — California transferred from Mexico | February 1848 |
| Gold discovered at Sutter's Mill, Coloma | January 24, 1848 |
| US military governance (no civilian territorial legislature) | 1848–1850 |
| **California statehood** (bypassed territorial status entirely) | September 9, 1850 |
| 27 original counties created at statehood | 1850 |

**Research implication:** Because California went directly from military governance to statehood, there are **no territorial-era civil records**. The gap from 1848–1850 is particularly sparse — look to ship manifests, overland diaries, and military records for that window.

---

## Record Classes, Availability & Access

### 1. 🗓️ Census Records — **HIGH PRIORITY**

| Record | Coverage | Access | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| US Federal Census, 1850 | Statewide | Indexed — Ancestry, FamilySearch | **Partially destroyed** — several county returns are missing entirely |
| California State Census, 1852 | Statewide | **88% name-indexed + full-text searchable** on FamilySearch (IGN 006601001, 522 images) | Best single substitute for the damaged 1850 census |
| US Federal Census, 1860 | Statewide | Indexed — Ancestry, FamilySearch | Fully intact; captures the post-Rush settled population |

> ⚠️ **Critical quirk:** The 1850 federal census for California is only partially intact — some counties' returns were never received or were destroyed. The **1852 California State Census** is the essential substitute and is well-indexed.

---

### 2. ⚓ Ship Passenger Manifests — **HIGH PRIORITY for sea-route migrants**

| Record | Coverage | Access |
|---|---|---|
| Port Arrival Registers, Sacramento | 1849–1858 | **Browse-only** (0% indexed, not full-text) — FamilySearch IGN 006601003, 298 images |
| San Francisco port arrivals | 1849+ | Physical/microfilm at **National Archives, San Bruno (Pacific Region)** |

- The majority of "Forty-Niners" arrived by sea via Cape Horn or the Panama route — passenger manifests are a first-line source.
- San Francisco was the primary landing port; some arrivals came via Sacramento River steamers.
- Browse image-by-image — no name index exists for the digitized volumes. Plan time accordingly.
- NARA San Bruno ([archives.gov](https://www.archives.gov/)) holds the authoritative federal copies of passenger manifests.

---

### 3. ⛏️ Mining Claim Records — **UNIQUE to this research context**

| Record | Coverage | Access |
|---|---|---|
| Mining Claim Registers, San Francisco | 1849–1855 | **Browse-only** (0% indexed, not full-text) — FamilySearch IGN 006601002, 340 images |
| County-level mining claims | Varies by county | Physical — county recorders' offices or California State Archives |

- Mining claims were recorded at the **county recorder's office** for whichever county the claim was located in (e.g., El Dorado, Placer, Tuolumne, Calaveras, Nevada counties were the major mining counties).
- Search by the **specific mining county**, not just "California."
- The FamilySearch digitized set covers San Francisco registrations; county-level registers require contacting individual county archives.

---

### 4. 📋 Vital Records (County-Level Civil Registration)

| Collection | Records | Access |
|---|---|---|
| California, County Births, 1850–1919 | 789,456 — **indexed + images** | FamilySearch collection [2017276](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2017276) |
| California, County Marriages, 1850–1952 | 567,890 persons — **indexed + images** | FamilySearch collection [1452832](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1452832) |
| County Deaths | Sparse for early 1850s | County recorders / California State Archives |

> ⚠️ **Critical quirk:** Statewide civil registration was not consistently enforced in early California. County compliance in the early 1850s was highly irregular — **do not assume an event didn't happen because no civil record survives.** Church records and newspapers are the primary backup.

---

### 5. 🗳️ Great Registers (Voter Registration) — 1866–1910

| Collection | Records | Access |
|---|---|---|
| California, Great Registers, 1866–1910 | 1,345,678 — **indexed + images** | FamilySearch collection [1923340](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1923340) |

- The Great Registers **begin in 1866**, outside the immediate Rush window — but they are among the richest biographical sources for identifying where a miner ultimately settled.
- Entries typically include name, age, height, occupation, birthplace, and county of residence — invaluable for linking a Gold Rush migrant to a later settled identity.
- Use them to confirm that a miner stayed in California after the Rush and to gather physical description details.

---

### 6. 🏡 Land Records

| Record Type | Repository | Access |
|---|---|---|
| Federal land patents (BLM GLO) | Bureau of Land Management | Free online at [glorecords.blm.gov](https://glorecords.blm.gov) |
| County deeds | County Recorder's Office | Physical; some digitized at county or state archives |
| Mexican-era land grants | California State Archives / NARA | Physical; Bancroft Library has manuscript copies |

- California is a **federal land state** — the BLM General Land Office records are the starting point for land patents.
- Many Gold Rush settlers filed for land after the Rush wound down; check patents dated 1855–1870 for arrivals from the early 1850s.
- Pre-statehood **Mexican land grants** (Ranchos) are documented in the California State Archives and Bancroft Library — relevant for families with roots before 1848.

---

### 7. ⛪ Church Records

- Civil registration gaps make church records especially important. Mining camp communities supported Catholic, Methodist, and other Protestant congregations by the early 1850s.
- **Catholic Diocese of Monterey** (later Archdiocese of San Francisco) registers survive for some communities.
- **FamilySearch Mission Records collection** ([1804372](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1804372), 98,765 records, indexed) covers 1769–1850 — useful background for California-native or Mexican-heritage individuals but predates the Rush influx.
- Denomination archives and local parish offices hold records not yet digitized.

---

### 8. 🪖 Military Records

| Record Type | Repository |
|---|---|
| Civil War service & pension files | NARA / [Fold3.com](https://www.fold3.com) |
| Mexican-American War service (some 49ers were veterans) | NARA |
| California state militia records | California State Archives |

---

### 9. 📰 Newspapers

- Mining towns had active newspapers almost immediately. The *Alta California* (San Francisco, from 1849) is among the most important.
- [Newspapers.com](https://www.newspapers.com/) and the **California Digital Newspaper Collection** (cdnc.ucr.edu) offer searchable historical California newspapers.
- Obituaries, mining news columns, and arrival/departure notices frequently named migrants by name.

---

### 10. 🪦 Cemetery Records

- [FindAGrave.com](https://www.findagrave.com/) has California cemetery transcriptions, including Gold Rush–era mining camp cemeteries.
- Many early mining cemeteries are poorly documented — local historical societies and county archives sometimes hold sexton records.

---

### 11. ✈️ Overland Trail & Immigration Records

- Migrants who came overland (via the California Trail or the Santa Fe Trail) left fewer paper records but may appear in **trail journals, diaries, and wagon train rosters** — the best collections are at the **Bancroft Library (UC Berkeley)** and the **California State Library (Sacramento)**.
- Naturalization records (pre-1906) were filed in any court of record — look in both federal district courts (NARA San Bruno) and California superior/county courts.

---

## Key Repositories

| Repository | What It Holds | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** (familysearch.org) | 1852 state census (indexed), county births/marriages, mission records, mining registers | Free online |
| **National Archives, San Bruno** (Pacific Region) | Ship manifests, federal court records, naturalizations, military records | In-person / NARA catalog online |
| **California State Archives** (Sacramento) | County vital records, state census, land records, court files | In-person; some digitized |
| **California State Library** (Sacramento) | Gold Rush manuscript collections, newspapers, biographical data | In-person; digital catalog |
| **Bancroft Library, UC Berkeley** | Extensive Gold Rush manuscripts, trail diaries, land grant records | In-person (appointment); some digitized |
| **BLM General Land Office** | Federal land patents | Free at glorecords.blm.gov |
| **Ancestry.com** | US Federal Census (1850, 1860), passenger lists | Subscription |
| **Fold3.com** | Military service records, pension files | Subscription |
| **Newspapers.com / CDNC** | Historical California newspapers | Subscription / free |
| **FindAGrave.com** | Cemetery transcriptions | Free |

---

## Researcher Quirks & Warnings

1. **The 1850 federal census is partially destroyed** — several California county returns are lost. Always check the 1852 California State Census as a substitute.
2. **Civil registration was inconsistent in the early 1850s.** County-level births and deaths were often unrecorded. Supplement with church records and newspapers.
3. **Gold Rush miners were highly mobile and transient.** Many left no local vital records in California — ship manifests and mining claim registers may be your only documentation of their presence.
4. **Search by mining county, not just "California."** Mining claims, local court records, and church registers are county-level; know where your ancestor mined (El Dorado, Placer, Tuolumne, Calaveras, Nevada, and Amador counties were the core Mother Lode counties).
5. **The Great Registers begin in 1866**, not during the Rush itself — but they are uniquely biographical and worth checking for any miner who stayed in California.
6. **Foreign-born miners** (Chinese, Chilean, Mexican, Australian, French) may appear in ethnic-community newspapers, consular records, or foreign-language church registers not held at California repositories — widen your search to include country-of-origin records.
7. **FamilySearch has 14 digitized volumes for this period** (volume search returned a first page of 3; additional volumes exist under the pagination token). The Mining Claim Registers and Port Arrival Registers are **browse-only** — plan to search image by image with no name index.
8. **Pre-statehood (1848–1850) records are thin.** US military governance left minimal civilian documentation; rely on ship manifests, Mexican-era records, and military rosters for that window.

---

## Suggested Search Sequence

1. **1852 California State Census** (FamilySearch, IGN 006601001, 88% indexed) — first stop
2. **1850 & 1860 US Federal Census** (Ancestry/FamilySearch) — bracket the decade
3. **Ship passenger manifests** (NARA San Bruno; browse-only Sacramento arrivals on FamilySearch)
4. **Mining claim registers** — FamilySearch San Francisco set (browse-only) + county recorder for the mining county
5. **County births/marriages** on FamilySearch (collections 2017276, 1452832)
6. **Great Registers 1866–1910** to confirm post-Rush settlement and gather biography
7. **BLM GLO** for land patents
8. **Bancroft Library & California State Library** for manuscripts, diaries, and biographical compendiums
9. **Newspapers.com / CDNC** for Alta California and local mining-town papers

Let me begin by establishing the jurisdictional context for New York City in the colonial 1700s.I have the standardPlace: **"New York, New York, United States"**. Now launching the full parallel survey.Now let me read the specific New York City genealogy wiki page found in the search, and also try the New York state wiki pages directly.I now have all the data I need. Let me compile and present the guide.

---

# Locality Research Guide: New York City, 1700s Colonial Period

**Jurisdiction:** New York County (Manhattan), Province of New York (British Crown colony, 1664–1776); post-Revolution State of New York (1776–1800)
**Target Period:** approximately 1700–1800
**Compiled:** 2026-07-23

---

## Jurisdictional Context

New York City in the 1700s meant **New York County (Manhattan)**. The five modern boroughs were not unified until 1898 — Kings County (Brooklyn), Queens County, Richmond County (Staten Island), and Westchester County (which then included the Bronx) were entirely separate jurisdictions. Research in those areas requires their own county-level approach.

The city was founded as Dutch **New Amsterdam** (1626–1664) and captured by the English in 1664, becoming **New York** under the Province of New York. During the 1700s, it served as the provincial capital under British royal government. The Revolution (1775–1783) split the period: British occupation of NYC lasted until November 1783, making some loyalist record-keeping unusually continuous through the war. After independence, New York County records passed into the new state structure.

**Critical implication:** There was **no civil registration** of births, marriages, or deaths in colonial New York. Church registers are the *only* vital records source for the entire 1700s. Do not assume a civil record exists — it does not.

---

## Record Types, Access, and Repositories

### 1. 🏛️ Church Records — **PRIME SOURCE**
**Access: Indexed (FamilySearch) + browse-only Dutch-language volume**

Church registers are the backbone of 1700s NYC research. The main denominations active in colonial New York City include:

| Congregation | Notes |
|---|---|
| **Dutch Reformed Church** | Oldest continuous series, beginning 1639. Registers through 1776 are in **Dutch**. |
| **Trinity Church (Anglican/Episcopal)** | Founded 1697; baptisms, marriages, and burials. |
| **First Presbyterian Church** | Founded 1716. |
| **French Huguenot (L'Eglise du Saint-Esprit)** | French-language registers. |

**FamilySearch Collection:** [New York, Church Records, 1640–1940](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1469062) (Collection 1469062) — 2,345,678 records, name-indexed and searchable, with images.

**⚠️ Browse-Only Volume — Dutch Language:** `volume_search` returned one digitized volume specifically for the target period:
- **Image Group 007733180** — Dutch Reformed Church Registers, New York City, **1700–1776**
- 211 images | 0% name-indexed | Not full-text searchable | **Language: Dutch**
- This volume must be browsed **image by image** with no search — and requires reading 18th-century Dutch script. It is not reachable via `record_search`.

**Physical repositories:** Reformed Church in America archives (Gardner A. Sage Library, New Brunswick, NJ) holds the master Dutch Reformed register series. Trinity Church Archives (Manhattan) holds its own originals. The New-York Historical Society and New York Public Library (Milstein Division for Local History and Genealogy) hold transcriptions and microfilm.

---

### 2. 📜 Probate Records — **HIGH VALUE**
**Access: Indexed (FamilySearch)**

Wills proved in colonial New York went before the **Prerogative Court of New York** — a province-level court, not a local city court. This means you search under "New York" (province/state), not specifically "New York City."

**FamilySearch Collection:** [New York, Probate Records, 1629–1971](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1542780) (Collection 1542780) — 987,654 records, name-indexed.

**Published index:** The New-York Historical Society published the *Abstracts of Wills* series (multiple volumes), covering colonial-era wills with full name indexing — a critical finding aid for the 1700s. Available at major genealogical libraries and on Google Books.

**Repositories:** New York State Archives (Albany); New-York Historical Society.

---

### 3. 🗺️ Land Records — **HIGH VALUE**
**Access: Indexed (FamilySearch)**

Colonial land in New York was held under patents and grants issued by the Royal Governor on behalf of the Crown. Land conveyances (deeds) and patents are among the most complete colonial records.

**FamilySearch Collection:** [New York, Land Records, 1630–1975](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2078654) (Collection 2078654) — 1,567,890 records, name-indexed.

**Key published resource:** *Calendar of New York Colonial Manuscripts: Indorsed Land Papers* (published by the NYSA) indexes provincial land grants. The New York Colonial Manuscripts at the New York State Archives (Albany) contain the original instruments.

---

### 4. ⚖️ Court Records — **MODERATE VALUE**
**Access: Largely physical/microfilm**

The **Mayor's Court** was the primary local civil court for NYC. The **General Quarter Sessions of the Peace** handled criminal matters. The **Supreme Court of Judicature** and **Court of Chancery** handled higher civil matters. These records name litigants, witnesses, debtors, and sureties — valuable for identifying residents and relationships.

The wiki search results (FamilySearch Wiki, "New York City Genealogy: Colonial Records") note these records are held at the **New York State Archives** (Albany). No FamilySearch-indexed collection for these court records appeared in the survey — treat them as **physical/microfilm only**.

The New-York Historical Society also holds some court record transcriptions and abstracts.

---

### 5. 💰 Tax Records — **LIMITED COVERAGE FOR 1700s**
**Access: Indexed (FamilySearch) — but only from 1799**

**FamilySearch Collection:** [New York, Tax Assessment Rolls of Real and Personal Estates, 1799–1804](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1678432) (Collection 1678432) — 123,456 records. This barely clips the target window (1799 onward only).

Earlier colonial tax/assessment lists survive for some years and wards, but they are not in an indexed FamilySearch collection for the 1700–1798 period. The FamilySearch Wiki search ("Tax and Military Records") notes that ward tax assessment lists exist for select years. These are held at the **New York State Archives** and the **New-York Historical Society**; some have been transcribed and published. Treat pre-1799 tax records as **physical/microfilm only**.

---

### 6. ⚔️ Military Records — **USEFUL FOR LATTER HALF OF CENTURY**
**Access: Physical/microfilm at NARA and NYSA**

- **Colonial Militia Rolls (Province of New York):** List men of military age by residence. Held at the New York State Archives. The wiki search results confirm these survive for portions of the 1700s.
- **French and Indian War (1754–1763):** Provincial muster rolls at NYSA.
- **Revolutionary War (1775–1783):** Service records and pension files at NARA (Washington, DC). Fold3 ([fold3.com](https://www.fold3.com)) has digitized many pension files. Loyalist claims filed with the British government (for those who fled or were displaced) are at The National Archives (UK), Kew.

---

### 7. 📰 Newspapers — **UNDERUSED BUT VALUABLE**
**Access: Partially online**

The *New-York Gazette* (founded 1725) and *New-York Weekly Journal* (1733) are the earliest surviving colonial papers. Notices of deaths, estate sales, runaway servants and enslaved persons, ship arrivals, and business transactions all name individuals.

- [Chronicling America](https://chroniclingamerica.loc.gov/) (Library of Congress) — free
- [Genealogy Bank](https://www.genealogybank.com) — subscription
- [Newspapers.com](https://www.newspapers.com) — subscription

---

### 8. 📋 New York Colonial Manuscripts
**Access: Physical at NYSA; published calendar**

The **New York Colonial Manuscripts** at the New York State Archives (Albany) are the official provincial government records — correspondence, patents, orders, council minutes — from the British administration. Researchers working on prominent families, landowners, or officeholders should consult these. The *Calendar of New York Colonial Manuscripts* (published by the State) provides an index.

---

## Key Repositories at a Glance

| Repository | What They Hold |
|---|---|
| **New York State Archives**, Albany, NY | Colonial Manuscripts, court records, militia rolls, land records |
| **New York Genealogical & Biographical Society (NYG&B)** | Colonial church transcriptions, published genealogies, searchable database |
| **New-York Historical Society** | Abstracts of Wills, court transcriptions, manuscript collections |
| **New York Public Library** (Milstein Division) | Church registers (microfilm), newspapers, published colonial records |
| **Reformed Church in America Archives**, New Brunswick, NJ | Dutch Reformed church registers (originals and transcriptions) |
| **Trinity Church Archives**, Manhattan | Anglican baptisms, marriages, burials from 1697 |
| **FamilySearch** (online) | Church Records 1640–1940; Land Records 1630–1975; Probate Records 1629–1971 |
| **NARA** (National Archives) | Revolutionary War service and pension records |

---

## External Links Found (FamilySearch-Curated)
The external links search returned 6 resources for this place, including:
- **Ancestry.com** — U.S. Federal Census (post-1790; not applicable to colonial period, but useful for context)
- **NARA** ([archives.gov](https://www.archives.gov)) — Revolutionary War military records
- **Fold3** ([fold3.com](https://www.fold3.com)) — Military pension files
- **FindAGrave** ([findagrave.com](https://www.findagrave.com)) — Cemetery records; Trinity Churchyard and other Manhattan graveyards have colonial-era stones
- **Newspapers.com** — Historical newspapers

---

## ⚠️ Critical Quirks for Colonial NYC Research

1. **No civil registration exists for the 1700s.** Church registers are the sole source of baptisms, marriages, and burials. If your ancestor wasn't baptized in a recorded congregation, the vital event may simply not survive.

2. **Dutch Reformed registers through 1776 are in Dutch.** The FamilySearch volume for 1700–1776 (Image Group 007733180, 211 images) is entirely browse-only — 0% name-indexed, no full-text search — and requires reading 18th-century Dutch script.

3. **NYC = New York County (Manhattan) only.** Brooklyn, Staten Island, and Queens were separate counties. A family in colonial Brooklyn is a Kings County research problem, not a New York County one.

4. **Probate was province-wide.** Wills were proved at the Prerogative Court of New York (a colonial province court), not a local NYC office. Search probate under "New York" broadly, not just the city.

5. **The FamilySearch County Marriages collection (1847–1936) does not cover the 1700s at all.** Do not expect to find 18th-century marriages there.

6. **The city was under British occupation 1776–1783.** Some Loyalist families fled; some Patriot records were disrupted. Loyalist claims are at The National Archives (UK). British Army muster rolls may name colonial residents.

7. **African American and enslaved persons** are documented primarily in church records (baptisms) and in newspaper runaway notices, slave sale ads, and estate inventories — not in dedicated registers. The *New York Manumission Society Records* (1785 onward) at the New-York Historical Society is the earliest systematic source post-Revolution.

---

## Summary Digitization Status

| Record Type | FamilySearch Status | Years Covered |
|---|---|---|
| Church Records (general) | ✅ Indexed + images (Col. 1469062) | 1640–1940 |
| Dutch Reformed Registers | 🟡 Browse-only, Dutch language (Vol. 007733180) | 1700–1776 |
| Land Records | ✅ Indexed + images (Col. 2078654) | 1630–1975 |
| Probate Records | ✅ Indexed + images (Col. 1542780) | 1629–1971 |
| Tax Assessment Rolls | ✅ Indexed (Col. 1678432) | 1799–1804 only |
| Court Records | 🔴 Physical/microfilm at NYSA | 1700s |
| Colonial Militia Rolls | 🔴 Physical at NYSA | Various years |
| Colonial Manuscripts | 🔴 Physical at NYSA; published calendar | 1664–1776 |
| Revolutionary War Records | 🟡 Fold3/NARA (partial digital) | 1775–1783 |
| Newspapers | 🟡 Partial online (Chronicling America, etc.) | 1725+ |

---

*Sources: FamilySearch collections survey; FamilySearch Wiki "New York City Genealogy: Colonial Records" and "Tax and Military Records"; Wikipedia articles on New York City and the Province of New York; FamilySearch volume search (Image Group 007733180).*

---

This is a standalone Q&A session with no active research project, so no data is persisted — but everything above is grounded in what the tools returned. When you're ready to build a research plan around a specific ancestor or question, I can run the `research-plan` skill to sequence searches using these record sets.

---

## Virginia — Revolutionary War Families Research Guide
**Time Period:** 1775–1783 · **Focus:** Military service and wartime activities

---

### Jurisdictional Context

During the target period, Virginia was transitioning from **British colony to American Commonwealth**. The Virginia Declaration of Rights and Commonwealth constitution were adopted in 1776; Virginia did not ratify the U.S. Constitution until **1788** (reflected in FamilySearch's date range for the state). Records created during 1775–1783 were maintained at the **county level** by county courts and at the state level by Virginia's General Assembly and executive. When searching, be aware that some finding aids catalogue early records under "Virginia Colony" vs. "Virginia (State)" — search both.

According to Wikipedia, Virginia contributed more troops to the Continental Army than any other colony, and key engagements — including the war-ending **Siege of Yorktown (1781)** — were fought on Virginia soil. County-level record-keeping began in the 1630s, making Virginia one of the best-documented colonial jurisdictions.

---

### ⚠️ Critical Research Warning: Missing 1790 Census

The **1790 federal census for Virginia was destroyed** when the British burned Washington in 1814. The **1782–1785 personal property tax lists** are the accepted genealogical substitute and are your primary tool for placing families in specific counties immediately after the war. FamilySearch's Virginia Tax Records collection (below) begins in 1782, squarely in the target window.

---

### Record Classes & Repositories

#### 1. 🎖️ Military Service Records
**The richest cluster of sources for this research question.**

| Source | Repository | Access |
|---|---|---|
| Continental Line service records & muster rolls | NARA, Record Group 93 | Physical / Fold3 online |
| Pension & bounty-land warrant applications | NARA, Record Group 15 | Fold3 / Ancestry.com |
| Virginia militia records | Library of Virginia, Richmond | Physical; partial microfilm |
| Virginia State Navy records | Published (Virginia State Library) | Print & some digitized |

**FamilySearch collection:** *Virginia, Military Records, 1775–1783* (ID 1945678) — 345,678 records with 172,839 images. ⚠️ *Volume-level search was not available in this survey session; indexed vs. browse-only status at the individual-volume level cannot be confirmed. Verify access on FamilySearch before assuming name-searchability.*
[Search on FamilySearch →](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1945678)

**Pension files (NARA RG 15)** are the single richest source for Revolutionary veterans — many applications include detailed statements of family history, birthplace, age, and witnesses. These are largely digitized on **Fold3** and partially on **Ancestry.com**.

---

#### 2. 📋 Tax Records (Census Substitute)
Virginia personal property tax lists from 1782 onward survive in nearly every county and are the **primary substitute for the missing 1790 census**. They identify free white males by name, county, and taxable property — essential for locating a soldier's family immediately after the war.

**FamilySearch collection:** *Virginia, Tax Records, 1782–1850* (ID 1456723) — 456,789 records / 228,394 images.
[Search on FamilySearch →](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1456723)

---

#### 3. 🏡 Land, Marriage & Will Records
Wartime and post-war land transactions, wills written before a soldier's departure, and estate administrations for those who did not return are all captured here.

**FamilySearch collection:** *Virginia, Land, Marriage, and Will Records, 1600–1900* (ID 1734285) — 2,567,890 records / 1,283,945 images. Date range spans the target period.
[Search on FamilySearch →](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1734285)

**Bounty Land — Virginia Military District of Ohio:** Virginia rewarded its veterans with land in what is now southern Ohio. Warrant and survey records connecting a veteran to his claim are held at the **Library of Virginia / Virginia State Library** (per the FamilySearch wiki article *Virginia Military Records*). These records can confirm service and post-war residence.

---

#### 4. ⛪ Church Records
Anglican/Episcopal parish registers were the de facto vital registration system before civil registration. Baptist, Presbyterian, and Methodist congregations also kept meeting records from this era.

**FamilySearch collection:** *Virginia, Church Records, 1680–1929* (ID 1598230) — 1,234,567 records / 617,283 images.
[Search on FamilySearch →](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1598230)

No statewide civil vital registration existed in Virginia during 1775–1783. Church registers and county court order books are your primary sources for baptisms, marriages, and burials in this period.

---

#### 5. 🏛️ County Court Records
County courts maintained **order books, deed books, will books, and minute books** continuously through the war years. These record wartime estate administrations, guardianships for soldiers' orphans, land sales by widows, and loyalty proceedings.

**FamilySearch collection:** *Virginia, County Court Records, 1650–1920* (ID 2134567) — 1,789,012 records / 894,506 images.
[Search on FamilySearch →](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2134567)

---

#### 6. 📰 Historical Newspapers
Virginia newspapers from the war period (e.g., the *Virginia Gazette*, published in Williamsburg) carried military appointments, casualty notices, and public announcements. Available on **Newspapers.com** and through the Library of Virginia.

---

### Key Repositories

| Repository | What It Holds | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **National Archives (NARA)** — archives.gov | RG 93 (service records), RG 15 (pensions/bounty land) | Online via Fold3 & Ancestry; physical in Washington, D.C. |
| **Library of Virginia**, Richmond | Virginia militia records, county court originals, state records | Physical; catalog at lva.virginia.gov; some digitized |
| **Fold3** — fold3.com | Digitized NARA military records, pension files | Subscription |
| **Ancestry.com** | Pension files, census substitutes, some Virginia collections | Subscription |
| **Find A Grave** — findagrave.com | Cemetery records; some Rev. War veteran graves noted | Free |
| **FamilySearch** | 5 Virginia collections overlapping 1775–1783 (above) | Free |

---

### Research Strategy: Recommended Sequence

1. **Start with NARA pension files (RG 15)** — if your ancestor served and survived, a pension application likely exists and may contain rich family narrative.
2. **Check FamilySearch collection 1945678** (*Virginia, Military Records, 1775–1783*) for direct service evidence.
3. **Use the 1782 personal property tax lists** (collection 1456723) to establish county location immediately after the war — essential since the 1790 census is lost.
4. **Pull county court records** (collection 2134567) for the relevant county to find wills, estate administrations, and guardianships connected to wartime deaths.
5. **Search church records** (collection 1598230) for baptisms, marriages, and burials of soldiers and their families.
6. **Check bounty land records** at the Library of Virginia if the ancestor claimed Ohio land — these confirm service and provide post-war location.

---

### Coverage Gaps & Cautions

- **Volume_search** (the tool for assessing indexed vs. browse-only status at the image-volume level) was not available in this survey. Record counts above are as returned by FamilySearch's collections tool; verify name-search availability for each collection on FamilySearch before assuming fully indexed access.
- **Virginia-specific wiki pages** for all four sections (home, getting_started, online_records, research_tips) returned only the generic FamilySearch research guide — no Virginia-specific quirks were surfaced via that tool in this session. The FamilySearch wiki article *Virginia Military Records* (found via wiki search) is your most targeted reference.
- **Civil War record losses:** Some county courthouses lost records during the Civil War. The Library of Virginia has compiled substitute lists for many affected counties — confirm record survival for your specific county of interest before concluding a search is negative.
- **"Colony" vs. "State" indexing:** Records created before 1776 may be catalogued under "Virginia Colony." Records from 1776–1788 may appear under either label depending on the repository.

---

This guide covers the five FamilySearch collections confirmed to overlap 1775–1783, the two NARA record groups most critical for military research, and the Library of Virginia as the primary state-level repository. The next logical step would be a targeted **research plan** for a specific ancestor, or a search of the pension files on Fold3 or Ancestry. Let me know how you'd like to proceed!

# Locality Guide: New Orleans, Orleans Parish, Louisiana (1800–1850)

## Jurisdiction overview

- **Formed:** City founded 1718 by the French; Orleans Parish coextensive with the city
- **Administrative center:** New Orleans (seat of Orleans Parish)
- **Parent jurisdiction:** Territory of Orleans (1804–1812) → State of Louisiana (from 1812)
- **Population during period:** No population data returned by the place_population tool for this fixture; historical sources place New Orleans at roughly 10,000 in 1800, growing to ~100,000 by mid-century, making it one of the largest U.S. cities of the era
- **Economy:** Major port commerce, sugar and cotton trade, enslaved labor, maritime industry; the city was the gateway for goods moving along the Mississippi River
- **Ethnic composition:** Remarkably diverse — Creoles of French and Spanish descent, Anglo-Americans arriving after 1803, free people of color (gens de couleur libres), enslaved people of African descent, German and Irish immigrants (especially 1820s–1840s), and a significant Caribbean diaspora
- **Religious denominations:** Predominantly Roman Catholic (the established faith under French and Spanish rule); Protestant congregations (Presbyterian, Methodist, Episcopal) expanded rapidly after the Louisiana Purchase
- **Key historical events:** Great Fires of 1788 and 1794; Louisiana Purchase 1803; Battle of New Orleans 1815; repeated yellow fever epidemics (1796, 1817, 1819, 1822, 1829, 1832, 1837); hurricanes and seasonal flooding throughout

---

## Boundary and jurisdictional changes

New Orleans presents an unusually complex jurisdictional trail for the early 1800s because it passed through **three sovereignties** in rapid succession:

| Period | Sovereign | Administrative system |
|---|---|---|
| 1718–1763 | France | French colonial law, notarial system |
| 1763–1800 | Spain | Spanish colonial law (Cabildo); notarial system continued |
| 1800–1803 | France (briefly) | Reverted per Treaty of San Ildefonso |
| 1803–1812 | United States (Territory of Orleans) | Transitional; civil law retained |
| 1812– | State of Louisiana | Civil law (Napoleonic Code) retained; parishes, not counties |

**Critically for researchers:** Louisiana uses **parishes**, not counties, and operates under the civil law tradition (Napoleonic Code) rather than English common law. This means records created here — especially legal documents — are structurally different from those in any other U.S. state. "Successions" replace probate; "tutorships" replace guardianship; "interdictions" replace conservatorship. Search for these Louisiana-specific terms when consulting indexes.

---

## ⚠️ Known record losses — disasters and what survived

This is the central research challenge for New Orleans and deserves its own extended section, as it directly shapes where every category of record must be sought.

### The Great Fires of 1788 and 1794

According to the FamilySearch Wiki article *New Orleans Genealogy* (§ Records Surviving Disasters):

- The **1788 fire** destroyed approximately 856 buildings — roughly 80% of the French Quarter — along with many colonial government records
- The **1794 fire** destroyed an additional ~212 buildings
- Together these fires wiped out most of the French colonial civil documentation

**What survived despite the fires:**

| Record type | Why it survived | Where it is now |
|---|---|---|
| **St. Louis Cathedral sacramental registers** | Held by the Church, physically separate from civil buildings | Archdiocese of New Orleans Archives; partially on FamilySearch microfilm |
| **Notarial acts** | Notaries kept independent custody of their protocols | New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center (30+ million pages from 1734) |
| **Cabildo records** | Spanish colonial governing body's records were preserved | Louisiana State Museum / Louisiana State Archives |
| **Spanish colonial copies** | Spain maintained duplicate records in Seville | Archivo General de Indias, Seville, Spain |

### Subsequent flood and hurricane damage

Wikipedia confirms the city suffered repeated flooding from hurricanes and storm surges throughout the early 1800s. Archival holdings in the Louisiana State Archives and parish repositories experienced ongoing water damage. When a particular record set cannot be found, assume damage rather than non-creation.

### Yellow fever epidemic mortality records

The repeated yellow fever epidemics (1817, 1819, 1822, 1829, 1832, 1837) killed thousands and created a need for death registration that predates statewide Louisiana civil registration (not required statewide until 1914). New Orleans began local death registration under Spanish rule in the 1790s; this continued into the American period. Check both the **Louisiana Statewide Death Index, 1819–1964** (FamilySearch collection 1543892 — index only, no images) and church burial registers for epidemic-era deaths.

---

## Available record types

### Vital records (civil registration)

- **Start date:** Louisiana statewide civil registration did not begin until 1914. However, **New Orleans maintained local death records from at least 1819** (reflected in the FamilySearch Statewide Death Index, 1819–1964, collection 1543892)
- **What exists:** Death index from 1819 (index only, no images online — physical records needed for full entries). Births and marriages were largely a church function until the mid-19th century
- **Where held:** Louisiana State Archives, Baton Rouge; Orleans Parish Clerk of Court for later civil registrations; originals of early death records at the Louisiana State Archives
- **Gaps:** Significant. For births and marriages in the early 1800s, **church records are the primary source**, not civil registration
- **Pre-registration alternatives:** St. Louis Cathedral sacramental registers from 1731 (with gaps from the fires); Protestant church registers for denominations active after 1803; notarial marriage contracts

### Church records ⭐ (most critical source for this period)

- **Denominations:** Overwhelmingly Roman Catholic under French and Spanish rule; Protestant churches (Presbyterian, Methodist, Episcopal) appeared after 1803
- **Record types:** Baptisms (often with godparents and parents named), marriages (with witnesses), burials (with age and sometimes cause of death); confirmation records
- **FamilySearch collection:** **Louisiana, Church Records, 1718–1925** (collection 1612905) — this is the key indexed collection covering the entire target period, with images; the tool returned 987,654 records and 493,827 images (figures as returned; treat as approximate)
- **Where held:** Archdiocese of New Orleans Archives (1100 Chartres Street) holds the original parish registers; FamilySearch has microfilmed holdings; Catholic Diocese of Baton Rouge Archives holds some overflow
- **Language warning:** ⚠️ Records created before 1803 and many created 1803–1820 are in **French** or **Spanish**. Researchers must be able to read these languages or use a translator. Latin is used in some sacramental formulas
- **Access level:** Indexed + images on FamilySearch for the 1718–1925 collection; the Archdiocese archives require in-person or written access for original volumes not yet microfilmed

### Notarial acts ⭐ (unique to Louisiana — essential for this period)

Notarial acts are the backbone of Louisiana genealogical research in a way unparalleled in other U.S. states. Under French and Spanish civil law (and continued by Louisiana after statehood), virtually every significant legal transaction was recorded before a notary: property sales, marriage contracts, apprenticeships, manumissions of enslaved people, contracts of all kinds, and succession inventories.

- **Repository:** The **New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center** holds over **30 million pages** of notarial acts dating to 1734 (source: FamilySearch Wiki, *New Orleans Genealogy* § Records Surviving Disasters)
- **FamilySearch volume:** Image group **004990115** — 167 digitized images of **"Notarial Acts (surviving microfilm copies)"** covering Orleans, Louisiana, 1803–1820
  - **Access level: Browse-only** — 0% name-indexed, not full-text searchable; must be examined image by image
  - **Language:** French and English — check both language sections of each volume
- **In-person access:** New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center is the authoritative repository for records not on microfilm; their holdings are far larger than what FamilySearch has digitized
- **Why this matters for disasters:** The notarial archives physically survived the 1788 and 1794 fires because notaries stored their protocols independently. They are often the **only surviving documentation** of property ownership, family relationships, and business dealings from the fire-affected colonial era through the early American period

### Census records

| Census | Notes | Access |
|---|---|---|
| 1810 | First federal census after the Louisiana Purchase; Louisiana was Territory of Orleans | Indexed + images on FamilySearch and Ancestry |
| 1820 | First census as a state | Indexed + images |
| 1830 | Full free household census | Indexed + images |
| 1840 | Full free household census | Indexed + images |
| 1850 | First to name all free household members | Indexed + images |

- **Note:** The 1800 census does not cover New Orleans — Louisiana was still under Spanish/French control and outside U.S. jurisdiction
- **State/territorial censuses:** Louisiana conducted territorial and state censuses; consult the Louisiana State Archives for survivals
- **Substitute for gaps:** Tax assessment lists (see Tax records below) serve as annual "censuses" between federal enumerations

### Probate records (called "successions" in Louisiana)

- **Louisiana terminology:** Wills, estate inventories, and administration proceedings are called **successions**, not probate. Guardianships are **tutorships**
- **FamilySearch collection:** **Louisiana, Probate Records, 1756–1984** (collection 2078341) — covers the full target period; the tool returned 456,789 records and 228,394 images (figures as returned)
- **Where held:** Orleans Parish Civil District Court holds succession records; the Louisiana State Archives in Baton Rouge has microfilm copies and some originals; FamilySearch has digitized a significant portion
- **Value for disaster research:** Succession inventories list all assets including real property, enslaved people (by name, age, and sometimes origin), household goods, and debts — often naming family members not found elsewhere. These are invaluable substitutes when vital records are missing

### Land records

- **System:** Louisiana uses a French-origin system of **arpents** and **toises** (not the federal township-and-range system); land grants were issued under French, Spanish, and U.S. authority
- **Types:** Original French concessions, Spanish land grants, U.S. confirmation patents (after 1803), notarial sale acts, tax assessment rolls
- **Where held:**
  - **Bureau of Land Management (BLM) General Land Office** (glorecords.blm.gov) — U.S.-era patents and confirmations post-1803
  - **Orleans Parish Conveyance Office** — deed books from the American period
  - **Louisiana State Land Office** — Spanish and French land grants
  - **New Orleans Notarial Archives** — notarial acts of sale
  - **Archivo General de Indias, Seville, Spain** — Spanish colonial land grants (copies held there)
- **Language warning:** ⚠️ French and Spanish land grants require reading those languages

### Military records

- **Relevant conflicts:** War of 1812 / Battle of New Orleans (1815); militia service throughout the period; U.S. Army garrison records
- **Record types:** Service records at NARA; pension files; the Louisiana militia records (held partly at the Louisiana State Archives and partly at NARA); muster rolls for the Battle of New Orleans
- **Where held:** NARA (fold3.com has many digitized military records); Louisiana State Archives; the Louisiana Historical Association
- **External link:** Fold3 (fold3.com) — curated by FamilySearch for this place and period

### Cemetery records

- **Major cemeteries:** St. Louis Cemetery No. 1 (est. 1789), St. Louis Cemetery No. 2 (est. 1823) — the above-ground vault cemeteries distinctive to New Orleans; numerous Protestant cemeteries opened after 1803
- **Online coverage:** FindAGrave (findagrave.com) — curated external link returned by FamilySearch for this place
- **Note on flood destruction:** Low-lying New Orleans experienced repeated inundation of cemeteries; some burial records and headstone inscriptions are damaged or destroyed
- **Physical records:** Sexton records and burial registers held by the Archdiocese of New Orleans Archives (Catholic) and individual Protestant congregations

### Marriage records

- **FamilySearch collection:** **Louisiana, Parish Marriages, 1837–1957** (collection 1798054) — begins in 1837, **after** the early 1800s target window; the tool returned 567,890 records and 283,945 images (figures as returned)
- **For pre-1837 marriages:** Rely on St. Louis Cathedral marriage registers (within the Church Records collection, 1718–1925) and notarial marriage contracts at the New Orleans Notarial Archives

### Immigration records

- **Port of New Orleans** was the second-busiest U.S. port of entry in the early 19th century; enormous German, Irish, and Caribbean immigration
- **Record types:** Passenger arrival lists at NARA; naturalization records filed in Orleans Parish courts; passport applications; New Orleans Customs House records
- **Where held:** NARA (Ancestry.com's U.S. Federal Census Collection and immigration databases — curated external link for this place); fold3.com for some military/immigration records
- **Note:** Naturalization records from any court of record before 1906; Orleans Parish District Court naturalization files are held at the Louisiana State Archives

### Tax records

- **Types:** Property tax (assessed annually by Orleans Parish); personal property tax lists
- **Value as census substitute:** Annual tax assessment rolls name heads of household with property values — they fill the gaps between decennial censuses and substitute for the years (pre-1810) before New Orleans appeared in federal census records
- **Where held:** Louisiana State Archives, Baton Rouge; some years at the New Orleans Public Library Louisiana Division

### Newspaper records

- **Local papers:** *New Orleans Gazette* (from 1804); *Le Courrier de la Louisiane* (French-language, from 1807); *Louisiana Gazette* (from 1804); multiple papers throughout the period
- **Value:** Obituaries, marriage notices, legal notices (sheriff's sales, succession advertisements), ship arrivals
- **Where held:** Newspapers.com (curated external link for this place); Louisiana State University Hill Memorial Library; New Orleans Public Library Louisiana Division; Library of Congress Chronicling America (chroniclingamerica.loc.gov)
- **Language warning:** ⚠️ French-language papers (*Le Courrier*, etc.) require French reading ability; these often contain vital-event notices not published in English-language papers

### Records of enslaved people and free people of color

- **Manumission records:** Recorded as notarial acts; New Orleans Notarial Archives holds thousands of manumission documents for the early 1800s — these name the enslaved person, their age, sometimes their origin, and the enslaver
- **Succession inventories:** Enslaved people were listed by name, age, and sometimes origin in estate inventories
- **Sacramental registers:** St. Louis Cathedral recorded baptisms and marriages of enslaved people and free people of color — often with more detail than for white parishioners (naming African or Caribbean origins)
- **"Quadroon balls" and plaçage records:** Social arrangements documented in notarial acts
- **Free Colored Militia records:** The New Orleans Free Black Militia (active under Spanish rule and retained by Americans) generated service records; Louisiana State Archives

---

## Repository guide

### Online repositories

| Repository | Collections confirmed for this place/period | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** | Louisiana, Church Records 1718–1925 (coll. 1612905); Louisiana, Probate Records 1756–1984 (coll. 2078341); Louisiana, Parish Marriages 1837–1957 (coll. 1798054); Louisiana, Statewide Death Index 1819–1964 (coll. 1543892); Notarial Acts 1803–1820 browse-only images (vol. 004990115) | Free |
| **Ancestry.com** | U.S. Federal Census Collection (curated link confirmed) | Subscription |
| **Fold3** | Military records (curated link confirmed) | Subscription |
| **FindAGrave** | Cemetery records (curated link confirmed) | Free |
| **Newspapers.com** | Historical newspapers (curated link confirmed) | Subscription |
| **NARA (archives.gov)** | Federal records, passenger lists, naturalization (curated link confirmed) | Free (in-person/online) |
| **BLM GLO (glorecords.blm.gov)** | U.S.-era Louisiana land patents post-1803 | Free |

### Physical repositories

| Repository | Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center**, 1340 Poydras St., New Orleans | 30+ million pages of notarial acts from 1734; the single most important repository for early New Orleans genealogy | In-person; research services |
| **Archdiocese of New Orleans Archives**, 1100 Chartres St. | Original St. Louis Cathedral sacramental registers; other parish registers | In-person; written request |
| **Louisiana State Archives**, Baton Rouge | Territorial and state records; tax lists; some notarial microfilm; succession records; naturalization files | In-person; some online requests |
| **Orleans Parish Clerk of Court / Civil District Court** | Succession files; civil court records; later civil registration | In-person |
| **New Orleans Public Library, Louisiana Division** | Local newspapers; city directories; some land records | In-person; some digital |
| **Louisiana State Museum (The Cabildo)**, Jackson Square | Spanish colonial Cabildo records | In-person |
| **Archivo General de Indias**, Seville, Spain | Spanish colonial land grants, government correspondence, and copies of New Orleans records from the 1763–1803 Spanish period | In-person; some digitized at pares.mcu.es |

---

## Records NOT online (physical visit or correspondence required)

- Original St. Louis Cathedral sacramental volumes not yet microfilmed (Archdiocese Archives)
- Pre-1803 notarial acts not captured in the FamilySearch 1803–1820 microfilm (New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center)
- Orleans Parish tax assessment rolls (Louisiana State Archives)
- Original Orleans Parish succession case files (Civil District Court)
- New Orleans Free Colored Militia records (Louisiana State Archives)
- Spanish-era Cabildo administrative records (Louisiana State Museum)
- French and Spanish colonial records held in Spain (Archivo General de Indias)

---

## Known record losses — summary table

| Disaster | Date | Records affected | Substitutes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Great Fire | 1788 | Most French colonial civil records; ~80% of French Quarter buildings and contents | St. Louis Cathedral registers (survived); Spanish Cabildo records; notarial archives (survived) |
| Great Fire | 1794 | Additional civil and commercial records | Notarial acts; Spanish archives in Seville |
| Yellow fever epidemics | 1817–1837+ | Death records sparse or absent for epidemic peaks | Church burial registers; Louisiana Death Index 1819+ |
| Hurricane/flood damage | Recurring | Periodic damage to courthouse and archive holdings | Cross-reference with notarial archives, church records |

---

## Research tips

1. **Start with church records, not civil registration.** Louisiana did not require statewide birth and death registration until 1914. For the early 1800s, the sacramental registers of St. Louis Cathedral (FamilySearch collection 1612905, *Louisiana, Church Records, 1718–1925*) are your primary vital-record substitute.

2. **Search the Notarial Archives early.** The New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center is an irreplaceable repository. The FamilySearch volume 004990115 covers only 1803–1820 (167 images, browse-only, French/English) — a tiny fraction of the notarial holdings. Plan an in-person or correspondence visit for records outside that window.

3. **Use Louisiana civil law terminology in indexes.** Search for "succession" not "probate," "tutorship" not "guardianship," "interdiction" not "conservatorship." An index built for common-law states may not cross-reference these terms.

4. **The 1837 cutoff matters.** The *Louisiana, Parish Marriages* collection (1798054) begins in 1837, missing the first four decades of American administration. For pre-1837 marriages, go directly to church registers within the Church Records collection.

5. **Read both French and English.** Records through at least 1820 are frequently in French, and Spanish-language records survive from the 1763–1800 Spanish period. The FamilySearch notarial volume is flagged as bilingual (French and English). If you cannot read French, consider the Archdiocese Archives' finding aids and published transcription projects.

6. **Check Spain for colonial-period records.** The Archivo General de Indias in Seville holds Spanish colonial administrative correspondence, land grant records, and duplicates of New Orleans records from the 1763–1803 period. Many have been partially digitized at pares.mcu.es.

7. **The Death Index (1819–1964) is index-only.** FamilySearch collection 1543892 (*Louisiana, Statewide Death Index*) has no images — it is a finding aid pointing to original death registers. Retrieve the originals from the Louisiana State Archives.

8. **Newspapers are critical for legal notices.** Under civil law, succession openings, property sales, and debt actions were advertised in local newspapers. French-language papers like *Le Courrier de la Louisiane* published notices not covered in English papers.

9. **Track ancestors across all three sovereignties.** An ancestor present in New Orleans in 1800 may appear in Spanish colonial records (in Seville), French territorial records (at the Louisiana State Archives), and U.S. territorial/state records — all for the same decade. Build the jurisdictional trail chronologically.

10. **Free people of color require a dedicated search strategy.** New Orleans had the largest free Black population of any U.S. city before the Civil War. They appear in notarial manumission acts, St. Louis Cathedral registers (sometimes with African or Caribbean origins recorded), succession inventories, militia records, and plaçage agreements — sources that require specifically targeted searches beyond standard vital record databases.

---

Now let me persist this as a `localities` entry in the research project.The guide has been compiled from tool results and saved as `loc_001` in your project. Here is a summary of everything the survey found, with particular focus on your question about disaster survival and substitutes.

---

## Key Findings: What Survived Disasters and Where to Look

New Orleans is one of the most disaster-affected genealogical jurisdictions in the United States, yet researchers are often surprised by how much *did* survive. The critical insight is that the records that endured the 1788 and 1794 fires are exactly the ones that matter most for early-1800s research.

### The Disaster Landscape

The **Great Fires of 1788 and 1794** destroyed roughly 80% of the French Quarter and with it most French colonial civil documentation. Subsequent **yellow fever epidemics** (1817, 1819, 1822, 1829, 1832, 1837) and **recurring hurricane flooding** caused further, ongoing damage to later archival holdings throughout the early 1800s.

### What Survived — and Where

| Record | Survival reason | Where to find it |
|---|---|---|
| **St. Louis Cathedral sacramental registers** (baptisms, marriages, burials from 1731) | Held by the Church, physically separate from civil buildings | **FamilySearch collection 1612905** (*Louisiana, Church Records, 1718–1925*) — indexed + images; also at the Archdiocese of New Orleans Archives (1100 Chartres St.) for volumes not yet microfilmed |
| **Notarial acts** (property sales, marriage contracts, manumissions, succession inventories) | Notaries kept independent custody of their protocols — these are the single most important surviving source | **FamilySearch volume 004990115** covers 1803–1820 (167 images, browse-only — 0% indexed, French/English, image-by-image only); the full 30-million-page holdings from 1734 are at the **New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center, 1340 Poydras St.** |
| **Cabildo records** (Spanish colonial government) | Spanish administrative records preserved separately | Louisiana State Museum (The Cabildo, Jackson Square) and Louisiana State Archives, Baton Rouge |
| **Spanish colonial duplicates** | Spain retained copies of New Orleans records during its 1763–1803 rule | **Archivo General de Indias, Seville, Spain** — partially digitized at [pares.mcu.es](http://pares.mcu.es) |

### Key Substitutes When Primary Records Are Missing

- **No birth/death civil registration** (Louisiana didn't require it statewide until 1914): → Church registers are the substitute; start with FamilySearch collection 1612905
- **Missing marriage records pre-1837**: → The *Parish Marriages* collection (1798054) begins only in 1837; use the Church Records collection and notarial marriage contracts at the Notarial Archives
- **Death records in epidemic years**: → The *Louisiana Statewide Death Index, 1819–1964* (collection 1543892) is **index only** (no images); retrieve originals from the Louisiana State Archives
- **Missing censuses** (no 1800 federal census covers New Orleans — it wasn't yet U.S. territory): → Annual tax assessment rolls substitute for intercensal years; at the Louisiana State Archives
- **Lost colonial civil records (pre-1803)**: → Notarial acts + St. Louis Cathedral registers + Spanish records in Seville

### Two Critical Cautions

1. ⚠️ **Language**: Records through at least 1820 are frequently in **French** or **Spanish**. The FamilySearch notarial volume (004990115) is explicitly flagged as bilingual. If you cannot read these languages, translator assistance is essential — many vital-event details appear only in the French-language press and sacramental registers.

2. ⚠️ **Louisiana civil law terminology**: Searching indexes with common-law terms will cause misses. Use **successions** (not probate), **tutorships** (not guardianship), and **interdictions** (not conservatorship) when consulting any Louisiana record index.

entry.guide_markdown:
# Locality Guide: New Orleans, Orleans Parish, Louisiana (1800–1850)

## Jurisdiction overview
- **Formed:** City founded 1718 by the French; Orleans Parish coextensive with the city
- **Administrative center:** New Orleans (seat of Orleans Parish)
- **Parent jurisdiction:** Territory of Orleans (1804–1812) → State of Louisiana (from 1812)
- **Economy:** Major port commerce, sugar and cotton trade, enslaved labor, maritime industry
- **Ethnic composition:** Creoles of French/Spanish descent, Anglo-Americans (post-1803), free people of color, enslaved people of African descent, German and Irish immigrants, Caribbean diaspora
- **Religious denominations:** Predominantly Roman Catholic; Protestant congregations expanded after 1803
- **Key historical events:** Great Fires of 1788 and 1794; Louisiana Purchase 1803; Battle of New Orleans 1815; repeated yellow fever epidemics (1796, 1817, 1819, 1822, 1829, 1832, 1837)

## Boundary and jurisdictional changes
New Orleans passed through three sovereignties in rapid succession:
| Period | Sovereign |
|---|---|
| 1718–1763 | France |
| 1763–1800 | Spain (Cabildo) |
| 1800–1803 | France (brief reversion) |
| 1803–1812 | United States (Territory of Orleans) |
| 1812– | State of Louisiana (civil law retained) |

Louisiana uses **parishes** (not counties) and operates under the **civil law tradition** (Napoleonic Code). Legal records use unique terminology: successions (probate), tutorships (guardianship), interdictions (conservatorship).

## ⚠️ Known record losses — disasters and what survived

### Great Fires of 1788 and 1794
Per FamilySearch Wiki (*New Orleans Genealogy* § Records Surviving Disasters):
- 1788 fire destroyed ~856 buildings (~80% of French Quarter) and many colonial government records
- 1794 fire destroyed an additional ~212 buildings

**What survived:**
| Record type | Why it survived | Where held now |
|---|---|---|
| St. Louis Cathedral sacramental registers | Held separately by the Church | Archdiocese of New Orleans Archives; FamilySearch microfilm |
| Notarial acts | Notaries kept independent custody | New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center (30M+ pages from 1734) |
| Cabildo records | Spanish governing body records preserved | Louisiana State Museum / Louisiana State Archives |
| Spanish colonial copies | Spain maintained duplicates | Archivo General de Indias, Seville, Spain |

### Yellow fever epidemics and flooding
Repeated epidemics (1817–1837) and hurricane flooding caused ongoing archival damage. When a record cannot be found, assume damage rather than non-creation.

## Available record types

### Vital records (civil registration)
- Statewide registration did not begin until 1914
- New Orleans maintained local death records from at least 1819 (FamilySearch collection 1543892 — **index only, no images**)
- For births and marriages: **church records are the primary source**

### Church records ⭐
- FamilySearch collection 1612905 (*Louisiana, Church Records, 1718–1925*) — indexed + images; covers the full target period
- St. Louis Cathedral registers from 1731 (with gaps) — baptisms, marriages, burials
- ⚠️ Records pre-1803 and many 1803–1820 are in **French or Spanish**
- Archdiocese of New Orleans Archives holds originals not yet microfilmed

### Notarial acts ⭐
- The backbone of Louisiana genealogical research — documents property sales, marriage contracts, manumissions, successions
- New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center: 30+ million pages from 1734
- FamilySearch volume 004990115: 167 images, Notarial Acts 1803–1820 — **browse-only** (0% indexed, not full-text searchable), bilingual French/English
- For records outside 1803–1820: in-person or correspondence visit to Notarial Archives required

### Census records
| Census | Notes |
|---|---|
| 1810 | First federal census; Louisiana was Territory of Orleans |
| 1820–1850 | Full federal censuses available; indexed + images |
- No 1800 federal census (New Orleans outside U.S. jurisdiction)
- Tax assessment rolls substitute for inter-census years

### Probate records (successions)
- FamilySearch collection 2078341 (*Louisiana, Probate Records, 1756–1984*) — covers target period; indexed + images
- Orleans Parish Civil District Court holds succession files; Louisiana State Archives has microfilm
- Succession inventories list enslaved people by name, age, and sometimes origin

### Marriage records
- FamilySearch collection 1798054 (*Louisiana, Parish Marriages, 1837–1957*) — begins 1837, **after** the main target window
- For pre-1837 marriages: St. Louis Cathedral registers (within collection 1612905) and notarial marriage contracts

### Land records
- French arpent system (not township-and-range)
- BLM GLO (glorecords.blm.gov) for U.S.-era patents post-1803; Orleans Parish Conveyance Office for deed books; Spanish/French grants at Louisiana State Land Office and Archivo General de Indias
- Notarial sale acts at New Orleans Notarial Archives

### Military records
- War of 1812 / Battle of New Orleans (1815); Louisiana militia; U.S. Army garrison records
- NARA (fold3.com); Louisiana State Archives; Louisiana Historical Association

### Cemetery records
- St. Louis Cemetery No. 1 (est. 1789), St. Louis Cemetery No. 2 (est. 1823); Protestant cemeteries post-1803
- FindAGrave (findagrave.com)
- Sexton records: Archdiocese of New Orleans Archives (Catholic); individual Protestant congregations

### Newspaper records
- *New Orleans Gazette* (from 1804); *Le Courrier de la Louisiane* (French, from 1807); *Louisiana Gazette* (from 1804)
- Newspapers.com; LSU Hill Memorial Library; New Orleans Public Library Louisiana Division; Chronicling America
- ⚠️ French-language papers contain vital-event notices not published in English papers

### Records of enslaved people and free people of color
- Notarial manumission acts (New Orleans Notarial Archives)
- St. Louis Cathedral registers — often record African/Caribbean origins
- Succession inventories — name enslaved people with age and sometimes origin
- Free Colored Militia records (Louisiana State Archives)

### Tax records
- Annual property tax/personal property assessments substitute for inter-census years
- Louisiana State Archives, Baton Rouge; New Orleans Public Library Louisiana Division

## Repository guide

### Online repositories
| Repository | Collections | Access |
|---|---|---|
| FamilySearch | Church Records 1718–1925; Probate Records 1756–1984; Parish Marriages 1837–1957; Death Index 1819–1964; Notarial Acts 1803–1820 (browse-only) | Free |
| Ancestry.com | U.S. Federal Census Collection | Subscription |
| Fold3 | Military records | Subscription |
| FindAGrave | Cemetery records | Free |
| Newspapers.com | Historical newspapers | Subscription |
| NARA (archives.gov) | Federal records, passenger lists, naturalization | Free |
| BLM GLO | U.S.-era Louisiana land patents post-1803 | Free |

### Physical repositories
| Repository | Holdings |
|---|---|
| New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center, 1340 Poydras St. | 30M+ pages notarial acts from 1734 |
| Archdiocese of New Orleans Archives, 1100 Chartres St. | St. Louis Cathedral registers and parish records |
| Louisiana State Archives, Baton Rouge | Territorial/state records; tax lists; succession records; naturalization |
| Orleans Parish Civil District Court | Succession files; civil court records |
| New Orleans Public Library, Louisiana Division | Newspapers; city directories; land records |
| Louisiana State Museum (The Cabildo), Jackson Square | Spanish colonial Cabildo records |
| Archivo General de Indias, Seville, Spain | Spanish colonial records 1763–1803 (pares.mcu.es partially digitized) |

## Records NOT online
- Original St. Louis Cathedral volumes not yet microfilmed (Archdiocese Archives)
- Pre-1803 and post-1820 notarial acts beyond the FamilySearch volume (Notarial Archives)
- Orleans Parish tax assessment rolls (Louisiana State Archives)
- Original succession case files (Civil District Court)
- Free Colored Militia records (Louisiana State Archives)
- Spanish-era Cabildo administrative records (Louisiana State Museum)
- French/Spanish colonial records held in Spain

## Research tips
1. Start with church records (collection 1612905), not civil registration.
2. Visit or write to the New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center — the FamilySearch microfilm covers only 1803–1820 and is browse-only.
3. Use Louisiana civil law terminology: successions, tutorships, interdictions.
4. The Parish Marriages collection (1798054) begins 1837 — use church registers for pre-1837 marriages.
5. The Statewide Death Index (1543892) is index-only — retrieve originals from Louisiana State Archives.
6. Many records are in French or Spanish; translator assistance may be needed.
7. Check Spain (Archivo General de Indias) for Spanish colonial period duplicates.
8. Newspapers are critical for succession advertisements and legal notices — search both French and English papers.
9. Track ancestors across all three sovereignties (French, Spanish, U.S.) — records for the same individual may span all three.
10. Free people of color require targeted searches in notarial acts, sacramental registers, and militia records.

Free people of color appear in notarial manumission acts, St. Louis Cathedral registers (sometimes with African/Caribbean origins noted), succession inventories, and Free Colored Militia records — requires targeted searches beyond standard vital-record databases.
added.guide_markdown:
# Locality Guide: New Orleans, Orleans Parish, Louisiana (1800–1850)

## Jurisdiction overview
- **Formed:** City founded 1718 by the French; Orleans Parish coextensive with the city
- **Administrative center:** New Orleans (seat of Orleans Parish)
- **Parent jurisdiction:** Territory of Orleans (1804–1812) → State of Louisiana (from 1812)
- **Economy:** Major port commerce, sugar and cotton trade, enslaved labor, maritime industry
- **Ethnic composition:** Creoles of French/Spanish descent, Anglo-Americans (post-1803), free people of color, enslaved people of African descent, German and Irish immigrants, Caribbean diaspora
- **Religious denominations:** Predominantly Roman Catholic; Protestant congregations expanded after 1803
- **Key historical events:** Great Fires of 1788 and 1794; Louisiana Purchase 1803; Battle of New Orleans 1815; repeated yellow fever epidemics (1796, 1817, 1819, 1822, 1829, 1832, 1837)

## Boundary and jurisdictional changes
New Orleans passed through three sovereignties in rapid succession:
| Period | Sovereign |
|---|---|
| 1718–1763 | France |
| 1763–1800 | Spain (Cabildo) |
| 1800–1803 | France (brief reversion) |
| 1803–1812 | United States (Territory of Orleans) |
| 1812– | State of Louisiana (civil law retained) |

Louisiana uses **parishes** (not counties) and operates under the **civil law tradition** (Napoleonic Code). Legal records use unique terminology: successions (probate), tutorships (guardianship), interdictions (conservatorship).

## ⚠️ Known record losses — disasters and what survived

### Great Fires of 1788 and 1794
Per FamilySearch Wiki (*New Orleans Genealogy* § Records Surviving Disasters):
- 1788 fire destroyed ~856 buildings (~80% of French Quarter) and many colonial government records
- 1794 fire destroyed an additional ~212 buildings

**What survived:**
| Record type | Why it survived | Where held now |
|---|---|---|
| St. Louis Cathedral sacramental registers | Held separately by the Church | Archdiocese of New Orleans Archives; FamilySearch microfilm |
| Notarial acts | Notaries kept independent custody | New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center (30M+ pages from 1734) |
| Cabildo records | Spanish governing body records preserved | Louisiana State Museum / Louisiana State Archives |
| Spanish colonial copies | Spain maintained duplicates | Archivo General de Indias, Seville, Spain |

### Yellow fever epidemics and flooding
Repeated epidemics (1817–1837) and hurricane flooding caused ongoing archival damage. When a record cannot be found, assume damage rather than non-creation.

## Available record types

### Vital records (civil registration)
- Statewide registration did not begin until 1914
- New Orleans maintained local death records from at least 1819 (FamilySearch collection 1543892 — **index only, no images**)
- For births and marriages: **church records are the primary source**

### Church records ⭐
- FamilySearch collection 1612905 (*Louisiana, Church Records, 1718–1925*) — indexed + images; covers the full target period
- St. Louis Cathedral registers from 1731 (with gaps) — baptisms, marriages, burials
- ⚠️ Records pre-1803 and many 1803–1820 are in **French or Spanish**
- Archdiocese of New Orleans Archives holds originals not yet microfilmed

### Notarial acts ⭐
- The backbone of Louisiana genealogical research — documents property sales, marriage contracts, manumissions, successions
- New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center: 30+ million pages from 1734
- FamilySearch volume 004990115: 167 images, Notarial Acts 1803–1820 — **browse-only** (0% indexed, not full-text searchable), bilingual French/English
- For records outside 1803–1820: in-person or correspondence visit to Notarial Archives required

### Census records
| Census | Notes |
|---|---|
| 1810 | First federal census; Louisiana was Territory of Orleans |
| 1820–1850 | Full federal censuses available; indexed + images |
- No 1800 federal census (New Orleans outside U.S. jurisdiction)
- Tax assessment rolls substitute for inter-census years

### Probate records (successions)
- FamilySearch collection 2078341 (*Louisiana, Probate Records, 1756–1984*) — covers target period; indexed + images
- Orleans Parish Civil District Court holds succession files; Louisiana State Archives has microfilm
- Succession inventories list enslaved people by name, age, and sometimes origin

### Marriage records
- FamilySearch collection 1798054 (*Louisiana, Parish Marriages, 1837–1957*) — begins 1837, **after** the main target window
- For pre-1837 marriages: St. Louis Cathedral registers (within collection 1612905) and notarial marriage contracts

### Land records
- French arpent system (not township-and-range)
- BLM GLO (glorecords.blm.gov) for U.S.-era patents post-1803; Orleans Parish Conveyance Office for deed books; Spanish/French grants at Louisiana State Land Office and Archivo General de Indias
- Notarial sale acts at New Orleans Notarial Archives

### Military records
- War of 1812 / Battle of New Orleans (1815); Louisiana militia; U.S. Army garrison records
- NARA (fold3.com); Louisiana State Archives; Louisiana Historical Association

### Cemetery records
- St. Louis Cemetery No. 1 (est. 1789), St. Louis Cemetery No. 2 (est. 1823); Protestant cemeteries post-1803
- FindAGrave (findagrave.com)
- Sexton records: Archdiocese of New Orleans Archives (Catholic); individual Protestant congregations

### Newspaper records
- *New Orleans Gazette* (from 1804); *Le Courrier de la Louisiane* (French, from 1807); *Louisiana Gazette* (from 1804)
- Newspapers.com; LSU Hill Memorial Library; New Orleans Public Library Louisiana Division; Chronicling America
- ⚠️ French-language papers contain vital-event notices not published in English papers

### Records of enslaved people and free people of color
- Notarial manumission acts (New Orleans Notarial Archives)
- St. Louis Cathedral registers — often record African/Caribbean origins
- Succession inventories — name enslaved people with age and sometimes origin
- Free Colored Militia records (Louisiana State Archives)

### Tax records
- Annual property tax/personal property assessments substitute for inter-census years
- Louisiana State Archives, Baton Rouge; New Orleans Public Library Louisiana Division

## Repository guide

### Online repositories
| Repository | Collections | Access |
|---|---|---|
| FamilySearch | Church Records 1718–1925; Probate Records 1756–1984; Parish Marriages 1837–1957; Death Index 1819–1964; Notarial Acts 1803–1820 (browse-only) | Free |
| Ancestry.com | U.S. Federal Census Collection | Subscription |
| Fold3 | Military records | Subscription |
| FindAGrave | Cemetery records | Free |
| Newspapers.com | Historical newspapers | Subscription |
| NARA (archives.gov) | Federal records, passenger lists, naturalization | Free |
| BLM GLO | U.S.-era Louisiana land patents post-1803 | Free |

### Physical repositories
| Repository | Holdings |
|---|---|
| New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center, 1340 Poydras St. | 30M+ pages notarial acts from 1734 |
| Archdiocese of New Orleans Archives, 1100 Chartres St. | St. Louis Cathedral registers and parish records |
| Louisiana State Archives, Baton Rouge | Territorial/state records; tax lists; succession records; naturalization |
| Orleans Parish Civil District Court | Succession files; civil court records |
| New Orleans Public Library, Louisiana Division | Newspapers; city directories; land records |
| Louisiana State Museum (The Cabildo), Jackson Square | Spanish colonial Cabildo records |
| Archivo General de Indias, Seville, Spain | Spanish colonial records 1763–1803 (pares.mcu.es partially digitized) |

## Records NOT online
- Original St. Louis Cathedral volumes not yet microfilmed (Archdiocese Archives)
- Pre-1803 and post-1820 notarial acts beyond the FamilySearch volume (Notarial Archives)
- Orleans Parish tax assessment rolls (Louisiana State Archives)
- Original succession case files (Civil District Court)
- Free Colored Militia records (Louisiana State Archives)
- Spanish-era Cabildo administrative records (Louisiana State Museum)
- French/Spanish colonial records held in Spain

## Research tips
1. Start with church records (collection 1612905), not civil registration.
2. Visit or write to the New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center — the FamilySearch microfilm covers only 1803–1820 and is browse-only.
3. Use Louisiana civil law terminology: successions, tutorships, interdictions.
4. The Parish Marriages collection (1798054) begins 1837 — use church registers for pre-1837 marriages.
5. The Statewide Death Index (1543892) is index-only — retrieve originals from Louisiana State Archives.
6. Many records are in French or Spanish; translator assistance may be needed.
7. Check Spain (Archivo General de Indias) for Spanish colonial period duplicates.
8. Newspapers are critical for succession advertisements and legal notices — search both French and English papers.
9. Track ancestors across all three sovereignties (French, Spanish, U.S.) — records for the same individual may span all three.
10. Free people of color require targeted searches in notarial acts, sacramental registers, and militia records.
