output.text_response:
Now I'll run all survey calls in parallel:Here is the complete locality research guide based on all tool results:

---

# Locality Research Guide
## Boston, Massachusetts — Immigrant Arrivals, 1840s

### Jurisdictional Context

Boston is a city in **Suffolk County, Massachusetts** (established 1630, standardPlace: *Boston, Suffolk, Massachusetts, United States*). In the 1840s it was one of the five major U.S. ports of entry, receiving enormous immigrant waves — particularly Irish fleeing the Great Famine (1845–1852). Wikipedia confirms that by 1850 over a quarter of Boston's residents were Irish-born. Immigrants settled in the North End and Fort Hill neighborhoods.

A critical date for this research window: **Massachusetts civil vital registration began in 1842**, so records from mid-decade onward were required to be filed with the state — but early compliance was uneven, especially in immigrant communities. Church records are often more complete than civil registers for the early 1840s.

---

## 🚢 Primary Record — Passenger Lists (Start Here)

### 1. Massachusetts, Passenger Lists, 1820–1891
- **FamilySearch Collection ID 1784312** — name-indexed, searchable at familysearch.org
- **Also indexed on Ancestry.com** (U.S. Federal Census Collection link confirmed via external links tool)
- **FamilySearch digitized volume 003345210** covers exactly **1840–1850**, Port of Boston — 389 images, **only 3% name-indexed but full-text searchable** → use the fulltext search function rather than browsing image by image
- **What 1840s lists record:** Name, age, sex, occupation, and country of origin *only* — no hometown or parish. This is a known limitation; supplementary research will be required to pinpoint a specific origin village.
- **Physical originals:** National Archives Record Group 36, held at the **National Archives at Boston, Waltham, MA** — per the FamilySearch wiki article "Massachusetts Immigration"

> ⚠️ **Key quirk:** The 1840s customs passenger lists do not record the immigrant's specific town or parish — only country of origin. Plan to corroborate with naturalization records, church registers, or the Boston Pilot database (below) to identify a precise Irish, German, or other European origin.

---

## 📜 Naturalization Records

### 2. Massachusetts, Naturalization Records, 1790–1906
- **FamilySearch Collection ID 1459213** — indexed
- Pre-1906 naturalizations could be filed in **any court of record** — U.S. Circuit/District Courts, Suffolk County Court of Common Pleas, or the Supreme Judicial Court. Don't stop at one court.
- **Declarations of Intention ("first papers")** and **Petitions for Naturalization** often record arrival date, port, and ship name — and sometimes birthplace more specifically than the passenger list does
- **Federal court records:** National Archives at Boston (Waltham)
- **State court records:** Massachusetts Archives (Boston) and Suffolk County courthouse
- **Also on Fold3** (military/NARA records confirmed via external links tool)

---

## ✝️ Church Records

### 3. Massachusetts, Church Records, 1630–1920
- **FamilySearch Collection ID 1921657** — indexed
- **Essential for immigrant families:** For Irish Catholics arriving in the 1840s, parish registers often predate or supplement sparse early civil registration
- Key Boston Catholic parishes from this era:
  - **Cathedral of the Holy Cross** — Boston's oldest Catholic parish
  - **St. Patrick's** — active from this period onward
- The **Archdiocese of Boston Archives** (Braintree, MA) holds the bulk of Catholic parish registers; contact them for an appointment
- Protestant registers (Episcopal, Methodist, Congregational, Baptist) are held at the **Massachusetts Historical Society** and partly microfilmed at FamilySearch

---

## 📋 Vital Records

### 4. Massachusetts, Town Vital Records, 1620–1890
- **FamilySearch Collection ID 2061550** — indexed
- Civil registration began **1 January 1842** in Massachusetts
- Boston city birth, marriage, and death records from 1842 are at the **Massachusetts Archives** and on FamilySearch and Ancestry
- Pre-1850 records also published in the **Massachusetts Vital Records series** (NEHGS), widely available in genealogical libraries
- Early 1840s coverage can be thin — cross-check with church registers

---

## 👥 Census Records

### 5. Federal Census, 1840 and 1850
- **1840 Census:** Names head of household only; other members counted by age/sex bracket — limited for immigrant identification
- **1850 Census:** First census to name every individual — a watershed source, taken at the height of Famine-era arrivals. Includes a **birthplace column by country** (Ireland, Germany, England, etc.)
- Both available on FamilySearch, Ancestry, and other platforms (confirmed via external links tool)

---

## 🗞️ The Boston Pilot "Missing Persons" Database — Highly Recommended

### 6. *Boston Pilot* Famine-Era Advertisements
- The *Boston Pilot* (Irish Catholic newspaper, est. 1836) published thousands of "Information Wanted" ads from families seeking lost relatives — many from the Famine era
- **Boston College digitized these** as the *Irish in America: Famine Immigrants* database — **free access at [infowanted.bc.edu](https://infowanted.bc.edu)**
- Many entries name the specific **townland and county of origin** — often the only way to identify an Irish ancestor's precise origin before you can search civil registration back in Ireland
- Per the FamilySearch wiki article "Massachusetts Immigration," this is a key supplementary source for this exact period

---

## 📰 Other Supplementary Records

### 7. Newspapers
- **Newspapers.com** (confirmed via external links tool) holds historical Boston newspapers with arrival notices and local news
- **Boston Public Library Microtext Department** holds extensive period newspaper runs on microfilm — in-person access

### 8. Massachusetts, Probate Records, 1635–1991
- **FamilySearch Collection ID 1678901** — indexed
- Suffolk County probate files are at the **Suffolk County Probate Court** and on FamilySearch
- Wills and estate inventories may name family members still in the home country

### 9. Military Records (Mexican–American War, 1846–1848)
- Some 1840s immigrants served in this conflict
- Service and pension records at **NARA** (Washington, D.C.) and on **Fold3** (confirmed via external links tool)

### 10. Boston City Directories
- List residents by name, occupation, and street address — useful for locating immigrants between census years
- Available at the **Boston Public Library**; partly digitized on **Ancestry.com**

### 11. Cemetery Records
- **Find A Grave** (confirmed via external links tool) indexes Boston-area burials
- Many immigrant Catholic burials in this era are in **Holy Cross Cemetery** (Malden) and **St. Augustine's Cemetery** (South Boston)

---

## Repositories at a Glance

| Repository | Key Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **National Archives at Boston** (380 Trapelo Rd, Waltham) | Passenger lists RG 36, federal court naturalizations | In-person; selected records on FamilySearch & Ancestry |
| **Massachusetts Archives** (Boston) | State vital records from 1842, state court naturalizations | In-person; some online |
| **Archdiocese of Boston Archives** (Braintree) | Catholic parish registers 1788–present | By appointment |
| **Massachusetts Historical Society** (Boston) | Protestant church registers, manuscript collections | In-person; some online |
| **Suffolk County Probate Court** (Boston) | Probate files | In-person |
| **Boston Public Library** — Microtext Dept. | City directories, newspapers | In-person; some digitized |
| **FamilySearch** (familysearch.org) | 5 indexed collections; browsable 1840–1850 passenger list volume | Free online |
| **Ancestry.com** | Passenger lists, census, city directories | Subscription |
| **Boston College** (infowanted.bc.edu) | *Boston Pilot* Missing Persons database | Free online |

---

## Research Strategy for the 1840s Boston Immigrant

1. **Start with the passenger lists** — FamilySearch Collection 1784312 and the full-text-searchable volume 003345210 (1840–1850). Expect only country of origin, not a village.
2. **Search the Boston Pilot Missing Persons database** — this often supplies the specific Irish origin that the passenger list omits.
3. **Pull naturalization records** from multiple courts — Declarations of Intention sometimes record ship, date, and a more precise birthplace.
4. **Find them in the 1850 census** — confirms presence in Boston and names all household members.
5. **Search church records** at the Archdiocese of Boston Archives — Catholic registers for baptisms, marriages, and burials that predate or supplement civil registration.
6. **Check city directories** (1840s Boston) to trace residence between census years.

---

*Sources: FamilySearch collections_search (Massachusetts, 1830–1865); FamilySearch volume_search (Boston, Suffolk, 1840–1850); FamilySearch wiki articles "Massachusetts Immigration" and "Massachusetts Church Records"; Wikipedia, "Boston"; external links survey (Massachusetts, 1830–1870).*

# 📋 Locality Research Guide
## Native American Tribes — Indian Territory (Present-Day Oklahoma) · Mid-1800s (c. 1830–1880)

---

### Jurisdictional Context

**Critical framing:** Oklahoma did not become a U.S. state until **1907**. During your target period the region was called **Indian Territory** — land set aside under the Indian Removal Act of 1830 for the forced relocation of Native American nations. It was a patchwork of sovereign tribal nations, not a U.S. territory with the usual civil infrastructure (no statewide vital registration, no standard county courthouse system, no federal census coverage in the ordinary sense).

The **Five Civilized Tribes** dominate the genealogical record landscape for this region:
| Tribe | Also Known As |
|---|---|
| Cherokee | — |
| Choctaw | — |
| Chickasaw | — |
| Creek | Muscogee |
| Seminole | — |

Dozens of smaller nations were also present (Osage, Quapaw, Ottawa, Wyandotte, Peoria, Miami, Seneca-Cayuga, Delaware, Shawnee, Potawatomi, Kickapoo, Sac & Fox, Comanche, Kiowa, Cheyenne, Arapaho, Caddo, and others), each with its own agency and roll history. The primary federal record authority is **NARA RG 75** (Bureau of Indian Affairs records).

---

### Repositories at a Glance

| Repository | What It Holds |
|---|---|
| **National Archives at Fort Worth, TX** | Core BIA records — RG 75 (tribal rolls, annuity rolls, agency correspondence, school records, land allotment case files). Primary repository for Five Civilized Tribes. |
| **National Archives, Washington D.C.** | Treaty documents, Bureau of Indian Affairs central office records (M234 microfilm — Letters Received by the Office of Indian Affairs). |
| **Oklahoma Historical Society (OHS), Oklahoma City** | Tribal newspapers, photographs, oral history, church records, local histories, and copies of many BIA rolls. |
| **Tribal Archives / Tribal Historic Preservation Offices** | Each tribe maintains its own archives — the Cherokee Nation (Tahlequah), Choctaw Nation (Durant), Chickasaw Nation (Ada), Muscogee Nation (Okmulgee), and Seminole Nation (Wewoka) all hold tribal court records, enrollment files, and newspaper archives. Access policies vary; contact each tribe directly. |
| **FamilySearch** | Indexed and digitized portions of tribal rolls, land records, and church records (see collections below). |
| **Fold3** | Military records including Civil War service (Union Indian Brigade), pension files, and some BIA correspondence. |
| **Ancestry.com** | Overlap with FamilySearch on census and Dawes-era rolls; also holds some annuity roll images. |

---

### Record Types & Digitization Status

#### 1. Tribal Enrollment / Census Rolls ⭐ *Core Source*

The most genealogically powerful records for this region. Rolls were compiled at different times for different purposes — work them in chronological sequence, not just the most famous one.

**Pre-Dawes rolls (mid-1800s — your target window):**

| Roll Name | Tribe | Approx. Date | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Old Settler Cherokee Roll | Cherokee | 1851 | Lists Western Cherokees who moved before forced removal |
| Drennen Roll | Cherokee | 1851–52 | Post-removal enumeration of Eastern Cherokees in Indian Territory |
| Chapman Roll | Cherokee | 1851 | Companion to Drennen; covers different subgroups |
| Duval Roll | Creek | 1832 | Pre-removal Creek enumeration in Alabama |
| Armstrong Roll | Choctaw/Chickasaw | 1831 | Pre/early removal roll |
| Various BIA annuity rolls | All Five Tribes | 1830s–1880s (annual) | Heads of household; sometimes family members listed |

**Post-1880 / bridge rolls (useful for confirming mid-century lineage):**

The **Dawes Rolls (1898–1914)** — though outside the mid-1800s window — are the definitive enrollment and explicitly record ancestry; they are essential for tracing backwards into your period. They include blood quantum and family relationships.

**FamilySearch collection confirmed by tools:**
- **"Oklahoma, Indian Censuses and Rolls, 1851–1959"** (Collection ID: 1987432) — 567,890 records, ~283,945 images — **indexed + images** (name-searchable). Covers the bulk of your period. ✅
- **FamilySearch volume 002298871** — "Tribal Enrollment Cards, Indian Territory, 1840–1870" — 940 images, **99% name-indexed**, full-text searchable. ✅

> **Search tip:** Search this collection at the tribal/roll level, not just by name — spelling of Native names was highly inconsistent, recorded phonetically by non-Native BIA agents.

---

#### 2. Annuity Payment Rolls *Core Source for 1830s–1880s*

Compiled annually by Bureau of Indian Affairs agents for each tribe to document annuity payments owed under treaties. Lists heads of household; sometimes enumerates family members and dependents. Critically, these predate Dawes by decades and are the primary substitute for vital records in this period.

- **Location:** National Archives, RG 75, Microfilm Publication **M234** (Letters Received by the Office of Indian Affairs, 1824–1881) and related series
- **Online access:** Partially indexed on FamilySearch and Fold3; much is **browse-only microfilm** — no name index, must scroll film by film organized by agency/year
- **Coverage gap:** Not all years survive for all agencies; gaps are common, especially for the 1840s

---

#### 3. Treaty Records

Treaties between the U.S. government and tribal nations define who counted as tribal citizens and what land rights attached. For the mid-1800s, key treaties include:

- **Removal treaties of the 1830s–40s** (e.g., Treaty of New Echota 1835 — Cherokee; Dancing Rabbit Creek 1830 — Choctaw; Pontotoc Creek 1832 — Chickasaw)
- **Post-Civil War treaties of 1866** — All five tribes who had Confederate alliances signed new treaties; these also established the status of **Freedmen** (formerly enslaved people held by tribal members who were then made tribal citizens)

**Location:** National Archives, Washington D.C. — Treaty series; also published in *Indian Affairs: Laws and Treaties* (Kappler, 1904) which is **freely available online** at Oklahoma State University's digital library.

> **Important:** Tribal Freedmen — African Americans who were members of the Five Civilized Tribes — have their own rolls (e.g., Cherokee Freedmen Roll, Choctaw and Chickasaw Freedmen Roll under Dawes). If your ancestor may be of African/mixed ancestry, search both the "by blood" and "freedmen" rolls separately.

---

#### 4. Land Records ⭐

Land allotments under the Dawes Act (1887, applied to Indian Territory 1898+) generated rich case files with family information. Pre-allotment, tribal land was communally held, so *tribal* land records (patents, improvements) are found in tribal archives and BIA agency files rather than county deed books.

**FamilySearch collection confirmed by tools:**
- **"Oklahoma and Indian Territory, Early Land Records, 1834–1907"** (Collection ID: 1541872) — 234,567 records, ~117,283 images — **indexed + images**. Covers your full window. ✅
- Also check the **Bureau of Land Management General Land Office (BLM GLO)** website for any federal land patents in adjacent territories.

---

#### 5. Mission and Church Records

Christian missionaries — Baptist, Methodist, Presbyterian, and Moravian — established schools and churches among the relocated tribes from the 1820s onward. These registers are the closest thing to vital records for the mid-1800s in Indian Territory. They record baptisms, marriages, burials, and school enrollment (sometimes including age, parents, and tribal affiliation).

**FamilySearch collection confirmed by tools:**
- **"Oklahoma, Mission and Church Records, 1820–1965"** (Collection ID: 1654321) — 123,456 records, ~61,728 images — **indexed + images**. ✅
- The **Oklahoma Historical Society** also holds extensive missionary correspondence and school records not fully digitized.

> **Coverage gap:** Survival is uneven — records of some missions were destroyed or never systematically collected. Browse-only microfilm exists for many denominations.

---

#### 6. Federal Census — Special Indian Schedules

Standard federal census did **not** enumerate Indian Territory residents in most years. However:
- **1860 & 1870 federal census**: Indian Territory was largely omitted or covered only sporadically
- **Special Indian Census Schedules** were taken in some years (1880 special schedules exist for some tribes)
- **Tribal censuses** were taken independently by BIA agents — these are the functional equivalent and are found in RG 75 / M234

> **Do not expect to find mid-1800s Indian Territory ancestors in standard Ancestry or FamilySearch census indexes** — they simply weren't enumerated there. Tribal rolls and annuity records are the substitute.

---

#### 7. Tribal Court and Newspaper Records

Each of the Five Civilized Tribes maintained its own courts, schools, and newspapers — often publishing in both English and the tribal language.

- **Cherokee Phoenix / Cherokee Advocate** (published Tahlequah) — held at Oklahoma Historical Society and partially online
- **Choctaw Telegraph** and others — OHS
- Tribal court records (citizenship disputes, probate, guardianships) — National Archives Fort Worth and tribal archives
- These records are largely **physical / microfilm only** — not fully digitized

---

#### 8. Military Records

The Civil War split the Five Civilized Tribes, with significant Confederate and Union factions. The **Union Indian Brigade** and **Confederate Indian units** both generated service and pension records.

- **Service records:** NARA (Fold3 has many digitized and indexed) — search under "Indian Territory" or the specific regiment (e.g., 1st Cherokee Mounted Rifles)
- **Pension files:** NARA; indexed on Fold3 and Ancestry
- Post-Civil War pension records may include family affidavits that reach back into the 1840s–50s

---

#### 9. BIA School Records

Federal Indian boarding schools (and tribal-run schools) kept enrollment registers listing student name, age, tribe, parents, and home. For the mid-1800s, the main school was the **Cherokee Male and Female Seminaries** and later the **Five Civilized Tribes mission schools**. Records are held at:
- National Archives Fort Worth (RG 75)
- Oklahoma Historical Society
- Individual tribal archives

---

### Key Research Quirks & Warnings

1. **Name spelling is unreliable.** BIA agents recorded Native names phonetically in English, and the same person may appear under multiple spellings across different rolls. Search broadly and compare ages and family members across rolls.
2. **"By blood" vs. "Freedmen" rolls are separate.** Under Dawes, tribal members were enrolled in distinct rolls by status. Searching only one will miss entire family lines.
3. **The Dawes Rolls (1898–1914) are post-period but essential.** They record blood quantum and explicitly identify which earlier roll(s) the individual's enrollment was based on — working backward from Dawes is the standard approach to reaching the mid-1800s.
4. **Indian Territory had no standard county courthouse system before statehood (1907).** Marriage, probate, and court records before 1907 are in tribal archives and BIA agency files — not county deed rooms.
5. **Tribal citizenship disputes are a rich substitute source.** Rejected applicants and citizenship court cases (1890s–1900s) often contain extensive family testimony reaching back to the 1840s–50s. These are at NARA Fort Worth.
6. **The 1866 Freedmen treaties created distinct citizenship lines.** Post-Civil War treaty signatories include provisions for formerly enslaved tribal members; their descendants appear on separate Freedmen rolls under Dawes.
7. **OHS holdings are underdigitized.** The Oklahoma Historical Society holds substantial manuscript and microfilm collections not available online — an in-person or mail research visit is often necessary.

---

### Recommended Research Sequence

1. **Start with Dawes Rolls** (1898–1914) — even though they're after your period, they anchor tribal affiliation and point back to earlier rolls. Search FamilySearch collection 1987432. Free at FamilySearch; also on Ancestry.
2. **Work backward through mid-century rolls** — Drennen (1851–52), Chapman (1851), Old Settler Roll (1851) for Cherokee; Armstrong (1831) and Duval (1832) for Choctaw/Chickasaw/Creek.
3. **Pull annuity payment rolls** for the intervening years (1830s–1880s) via NARA M234 microfilm — these are the annual "heartbeat" of BIA documentation.
4. **Check treaty documents** at NARA Washington or the Kappler online resource to understand what rights and rolls attach to your ancestor's treaty.
5. **Search mission and church records** (FamilySearch collection 1654321) for vital events.
6. **Check OHS and tribal archives** for court records, newspapers, and school files not online.
7. **For military ancestors:** Fold3 for Civil War service; NARA pension files for family affidavits.

---

### External Resources Confirmed Available

| Resource | URL | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| National Archives (NARA) | https://www.archives.gov/ | RG 75 — primary repository for BIA records |
| Fold3 | https://www.fold3.com/ | Military records, some BIA correspondence (subscription) |
| Ancestry.com | https://www.ancestry.com/ | Dawes Rolls, some annuity rolls, federal census (subscription) |
| Find A Grave | https://www.findagrave.com/ | Cemetery records — supplement for death dates |
| Newspapers.com | https://www.newspapers.com/ | Historical newspapers including Indian Territory papers (subscription) |
| Kappler's Indian Affairs: Laws and Treaties | https://digital.library.okstate.edu/kappler/ | Free full text of all treaties |

---

### Coverage Gaps to Note

- The FamilySearch wiki place pages for Oklahoma did not return Oklahoma-specific content — consult the FamilySearch Research Wiki directly for "[Oklahoma Genealogy](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Oklahoma_Genealogy)" and "[Five Civilized Tribes Records](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Five_Civilized_Tribes_Records)" articles.
- Annuity roll coverage in NARA M234 has gaps, particularly for the 1840s — some agency records did not survive.
- Smaller tribes (Osage, Quapaw, Potawatomi, etc.) have fewer digitized records than the Five Civilized Tribes; their BIA agency files at NARA Fort Worth are largely browse-only microfilm.
- No population data was returned for Indian Territory by the FamilySearch tool — expected, as the region falls outside standard U.S. state population tracking for this period.

---

This is no research project file to persist to, so the guide stands as a complete reference. Would you like me to now build a **research plan** for a specific individual or family, or dive deeper into any particular record type or tribe?

# Locality Research Guide: Pennsylvania Quaker Meeting Records, c. 1750–1800

## Jurisdictional Context

Pennsylvania was founded in 1682 by William Penn expressly as a refuge for Quakers (the Religious Society of Friends). By the late 18th century, Quakers were concentrated most densely in **Philadelphia, Chester, Bucks, and Delaware counties** — all in the southeastern corner of the state. The governing body for the region was the **Philadelphia Yearly Meeting**, which oversaw quarterly and monthly meetings throughout Pennsylvania, New Jersey, and the mid-Atlantic. There was **no statewide civil registration** of births, marriages, or deaths in Pennsylvania until 1906. For the late 1700s, Quaker meeting records are effectively the civil record substitute — and they are exceptionally detailed.

---

## ⭐ Primary Record Type: Quaker Meeting Records

These are the cornerstone of any Quaker family reconstruction for this period. According to the FamilySearch Wiki article *Pennsylvania Church Records*, monthly meetings routinely recorded:

| Event | What Was Recorded |
|---|---|
| **Births** | Child's name, parents, date — often more complete than a baptismal register |
| **Marriages** | Full meeting-house ceremony; the certificate was signed by **every attendee** — a gold mine of witnesses and kin |
| **Deaths & Burials** | Name, date, sometimes age |
| **Certificates of Removal** | Issued when a Friend moved to another meeting — essential for tracing migration |
| **Disownments** | When a member was disciplined or expelled; names family members and states the cause |

The **monthly meeting** is the workhorse record unit. Minutes and registers survive for many southeastern Pennsylvania meetings from the 1680s onward, well before the late-1700s target period.

---

## Repositories

### 1. Friends Historical Library — Swarthmore College *(Primary)*
The largest collection of American Quaker records in existence. Holds original meeting registers, minute books, certificates of removal, and correspondence for Pennsylvania monthly and quarterly meetings. Physical access required for unpublished originals; some finding aids are online.
- **Website:** [www.swarthmore.edu/friends-historical-library](https://www.swarthmore.edu/friends-historical-library)

### 2. Quaker and Special Collections — Haverford College
A major complementary repository, particularly strong for Philadelphia Yearly Meeting records and published Quaker genealogies.
- **Website:** [www.haverford.edu/library/special-collections](https://www.haverford.edu/library/special-collections)

### 3. FamilySearch — Digitized Collections & Microfilm

**Indexed collections on FamilySearch overlapping 1750–1800:**

| Collection | Date Range | Records | Images | Access Level |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| [Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1401638) | 1708–1985 | 1,245,830 | 607,220 | Indexed + images |
| [Pennsylvania, Civil Marriages, 1677–1950](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1571957) | 1677–1950 | 312,045 | 156,023 | Indexed + images |
| [Pennsylvania, Compiled Marriages, 1700–1900](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1502909) | 1700–1900 | 198,765 | None | Index only — no images |
| [Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1417683) | 1683–1994 | 893,214 | 446,607 | Indexed + images |

> ⚠️ **Church and Town Records (1401638)** is the most likely FamilySearch collection to contain Quaker meeting abstracts. Search it first for any names you're tracing.

**Browse-only digitized volume** (volume_search result):

| Volume | Coverage | Images | Access Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| Philadelphia Yearly Meeting Minutes (IGN: 001188322) | Philadelphia, PA 1750–1800 | 145 | ⚠️ **Browse-only** — 0% name-indexed, not full-text searchable |

This volume must be browsed **image by image** — there is no name search. It covers the governing yearly meeting minutes, so it's useful for context and committee appointments but less so for individual vital events (those are in the monthly meeting registers).

### 4. Pennsylvania State Archives
Holds colonial-era records including provincial tax lists, land warrants, and some church/meeting records on microfilm.
- **Online access:** [www.phmc.pa.gov](https://www.phmc.pa.gov)

### 5. Historical Society of Pennsylvania (Philadelphia)
Holds manuscript collections related to Quaker families and some meeting records not duplicated at Swarthmore or Haverford.

---

## ⭐ Published Finding Aid: Hinshaw's Encyclopedia

**William Wade Hinshaw, *Encyclopedia of American Quaker Genealogy*, 6 volumes** — abstracts Pennsylvania meeting records (and other states) into a searchable reference. This is the first tool most Quaker genealogists reach for. Check your local genealogical library or WorldCat for access. FamilySearch has also microfilmed many of the underlying monthly meeting registers that Hinshaw abstracted.

---

## Understanding Meeting Structure (Essential for Research)

```
Philadelphia Yearly Meeting  (annual; governance & discipline)
    └── Quarterly Meetings   (4x/year; 3–6 monthly meetings report up)
            └── Monthly Meetings  (the record-keeping unit — search here)
```

When tracing a family:
1. **Identify their monthly meeting** (e.g., Abington, Horsham, Uwchlan, Middletown, Falls, Wrightstown, etc.)
2. Search that meeting's **birth, marriage, and death registers**
3. Check for **certificates of removal** — if a family moved, both the originating and receiving meeting will have a record of the transfer

---

## Complementary Records for This Period

Because no civil registration existed, round out your research with these:

| Record Type | What It Covers | Where to Find It |
|---|---|---|
| **Provincial / Supply Tax Lists (1769, 1779–1793)** | Lists taxable inhabitants by county and township — useful for locating and dating family presence | Pennsylvania State Archives; FamilySearch microfilm |
| **Land warrants & surveys** | Pennsylvania Land Office records pre-date deeds; land patents name the original grantee | PA State Archives; [GLO website](https://glorecords.blm.gov) |
| **County deed books** | Property transfers, often naming heirs | County courthouse; FamilySearch microfilm by county |
| **1790 & 1800 Federal Census** | Heads of household only; place of residence | FamilySearch (indexed) |
| **Revolutionary War records** | Service, pension files, and pay vouchers — note: Quakers were often pacifists and may appear in *refusal* records rather than service records | NARA; Fold3 |
| **Probate records** | Wills and estate inventories name heirs explicitly | [FamilySearch collection 1417683](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1417683); county courthouses |
| **Newspapers (Pennsylvania Gazette, etc.)** | Death notices and family announcements | [Newspapers.com](https://www.newspapers.com); [Chronicling America](https://chroniclingamerica.loc.gov) |

---

## Key Research Tips

1. **No civil registration until 1906** — Meeting records ARE the birth/marriage/death record for this period. Don't expect county vital records to exist.
2. **Marriage certificates name witnesses** — the list of signatories on a Quaker marriage certificate frequently documents siblings, cousins, and neighbors — use it to map kin networks.
3. **Disownment records are not dead ends** — a Friend disowned for marrying out of meeting or bearing arms still appears in meeting minutes, often with family context that can't be found elsewhere.
4. **Certificates of removal track migration exactly** — when a family moved from, say, Chester County to newly opened western Pennsylvania, the removal certificate is often the only dated evidence of when and where they went.
5. **Search at the monthly meeting level, not the yearly meeting level** — vital events are in monthly meeting registers, not yearly meeting minutes (the digitized YM minutes volume is governance records, not a vital register).
6. **Dense Quaker counties to focus on first:** Philadelphia, Chester, Bucks, Delaware — if you don't know the specific meeting, start with the county-level collections on FamilySearch and Hinshaw before going to Swarthmore/Haverford in person.

---

## Summary: Where to Start

| Step | Action |
|---|---|
| 1 | Search **Hinshaw's Encyclopedia** for your surnames — it abstracts hundreds of PA meetings |
| 2 | Search **Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records** on FamilySearch (collection 1401638) |
| 3 | Browse the **Philadelphia Yearly Meeting Minutes volume** (IGN 001188322) on FamilySearch if your family was in Philadelphia/vicinity 1750–1800 |
| 4 | Contact **Friends Historical Library (Swarthmore)** for the specific monthly meeting registers |
| 5 | Supplement with **tax lists and probate records** to bracket dates and confirm residences |

Quaker records for this period are among the richest in American genealogy — the challenge isn't record survival, it's identifying the specific monthly meeting and then gaining access to the physical registers at Swarthmore or Haverford. Starting with Hinshaw and FamilySearch's digitized collections will carry you far before a repository visit becomes necessary.

---

# Locality Guide: Pittsburgh (Allegheny County), Pennsylvania — Late 1800s Steel Industry Era (c. 1860–1906)

---

## Jurisdiction Overview

- **City:** Pittsburgh, Allegheny County, Pennsylvania — a city from 1816, recorded on FamilySearch with the standard place **"Pittsburgh, Allegheny, Pennsylvania, United States"** (established 1758).
- **County:** Allegheny County, formed **1788** from Westmoreland and Washington counties. Records begin at county formation. The county is the key administrative unit for genealogical records — guide your searches at this level.
- **County seat:** Pittsburgh
- **Parent jurisdiction:** Commonwealth of Pennsylvania
- **Population note:** The FamilySearch population tool returned no fixture data for this period; however, per Wikipedia, Pittsburgh grew explosively during the steel boom — from roughly 49,000 in 1860 to over 300,000 by 1900, not counting surrounding mill towns also within Allegheny County.
- **Economy:** Steel production dominated. Andrew Carnegie's steel empire and rival firms operated massive mills along the Monongahela and Allegheny rivers, drawing the largest industrial workforce in North America.
- **Ethnic composition:** As documented in Wikipedia and the FamilySearch wiki, the mills attracted wave after wave of immigrants — **Irish, German, Polish, Slovak, Croatian, and Italian** workers were the major groups. Each formed distinct ethnic enclaves with their own churches and institutions.
- **Religious denominations:** Roman Catholic (serving Irish, Polish, Slovak, Croatian, and Italian parishes), Lutheran (German, Slovak), Presbyterian, Methodist, and Orthodox (Eastern European immigrants arriving in the 1890s).
- **Key historical events:** The Homestead Strike (1892) — a pivotal labor conflict at Carnegie's Homestead Steel Works — is documented in newspaper archives and government records and can help place ancestors at a specific mill at a specific time.

---

## Boundary Changes

- **Allegheny County** boundaries were stable throughout the late 1800s — no significant changes during 1860–1906 affect records access.
- **City of Allegheny** (across the Allegheny River from Pittsburgh) was a separate municipality until **1907**, when it was annexed into Pittsburgh. Ancestors living in Allegheny City before 1907 will appear in records under "Allegheny" not "Pittsburgh" — search both. Both were always within Allegheny County, so county-level records cover both.
- Pre-1788 records for this area were held under **Westmoreland County** and **Washington County** — relevant only for ancestors present before county formation.

---

## Available Record Types

### 🏭 Industrial and Occupational Records *(the core target for this research)*

This is the richest — and most fragmented — category for Pittsburgh. Per the FamilySearch wiki article **"Allegheny County, Pennsylvania Genealogy"**:

| Source | What It Contains | Access / Where Held |
|---|---|---|
| **Company employment records** | Payroll records, employee rolls, hiring/discharge dates, job classifications | **Fragmentary survival.** Some Carnegie Steel and successor firm records survive at the **Senator John Heinz History Center** (Pittsburgh) and the **Rivers of Steel National Heritage Area archives.** Contact each directly — these are not digitized online. Physical/in-person only. |
| **Union membership records** | Members' names, locals, dues records, sometimes addresses and birthplaces | Various labor unions; check the **Heinz History Center** and the **Historical Society of Western Pennsylvania** (part of Heinz). The Amalgamated Association of Iron and Steel Workers was the dominant union in this period. |
| **City directories** | Name, occupation, employer, and home address — annual publication | The **Carnegie Library of Pittsburgh** holds Pittsburgh city directories from the **1810s** onward. Also available on **Ancestry.com** (subscription). Excellent for tracking a worker year by year. |
| **Pennsylvania State Factory Inspector Reports** | Annual reports listing factories, number of employees, types of work, sometimes accident records | Published state documents; held at **Pennsylvania State Archives** (Harrisburg) and major library collections. Not digitized but available in print. |
| **Federal Census Industry/Manufacturing Schedules** | Non-population schedules listing factories, capitalization, employees, wages (1870 and 1880) | **Indexed + images** on Ancestry; **browse-only** on FamilySearch for some series. Cross-reference with population schedules to confirm individual workers. |

> **Strategy note:** Because surviving company records are fragmentary and scattered, the most reliable method for occupational documentation in this period is a **triangulation approach**: federal census (occupation column) + city directory (employer and address) + church record (ethnic parish identifies immigrant community) + naturalization record (origin country). Together these build a profile even when no direct employer record survives.

---

### Vital Records (Civil Registration)

- **Critical gap:** Pennsylvania did **not** have statewide civil registration of births and deaths until **1906**. The FamilySearch collection **"Pennsylvania, Death Certificates, 1906–1968"** (4,218,763 records — indexed + images) begins just after the late 1800s window. For deaths before 1906, **church records are the primary substitute**.
- **Marriage records (overlapping the target period):**
  - **Pennsylvania, County Marriages, 1885–1950** — 524,189 records, 1,048,378 persons, 312,045 images — **Indexed + images** on FamilySearch. Covers Allegheny County marriages from 1885 onward.
  - **Pennsylvania, Civil Marriages, 1677–1950** — 312,045 records, 624,090 persons, 156,023 images — **Indexed + images** on FamilySearch. Another marriage record series overlapping the period.
  - **Pennsylvania, Compiled Marriages, 1700–1900** — 198,765 records, 397,530 persons, **0 images** — name-indexed compilation only; no images to view. Useful as a finding aid but requires follow-up at the original source.
- **Birth registration before 1906:** No statewide birth certificates. Use **church baptismal registers** (see below) and the **federal census** (children's ages and birthplaces listed for parents).

---

### Church Records

This is the **single most important record category** for immigrant steel workers before 1906. Per the FamilySearch wiki, Catholic parishes served specific ethnic communities and their registers are often the best source for family data.

- **FamilySearch collection:** **Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985** — 1,245,830 records, 982,415 persons, 607,220 images — **Indexed + images**. This is the primary online source for church baptisms, marriages, and burials across the period.
- **Key ethnic parishes to identify:** Polish (St. Stanislaus Kostka, Sacred Hearts of Jesus and Mary), Slovak (several dedicated Slovak Catholic and Lutheran congregations), Croatian (St. Nicholas Croatian), German (various Lutheran parishes), Irish (St. Paul's Cathedral parish). Identifying your ancestor's ethnic parish is the most important first step — church records are organized by parish, not by surname.
- **Language of records:** Non-English records are common. Polish, Slovak, Croatian, German, Latin (Catholic sacramental registers). Researchers must be prepared to work with the original language or find a translator.
- **Physical repositories:** Original registers may be at the local parish, diocesan archives (**Diocese of Pittsburgh** or **Diocese of Greensburg**), or microfilmed by FamilySearch. Not all parishes are fully digitized.

---

### Census Records

| Census Year | What It Contains | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **1860** | Name, age, sex, birthplace, occupation, real and personal property value | Indexed + images — Ancestry, FamilySearch |
| **1870** | Same, plus father's and mother's birthplace | Indexed + images — Ancestry, FamilySearch |
| **1880** | Same, plus relationship to head of household | Indexed + images — Ancestry, FamilySearch |
| **1890** | Mostly destroyed in 1921 fire | Only a fragment survives; the **1890 Veterans Schedule** (Union veterans) partially compensates |
| **1900** | Same as 1880, plus years in the U.S., naturalization status, number of children | Indexed + images — Ancestry, FamilySearch |
| **1870 & 1880 Manufacturing Schedules** | Factory name, location, capital, employees, wages, products — not names of individual workers | Browse-only on FamilySearch; Indexed on Ancestry. Cross-reference with population schedules. |
| **1880 Mortality Schedule** | Deaths in the year preceding June 1880 — name, age, cause of death | Indexed + images — Ancestry, FamilySearch |

---

### Naturalization Records

Critical for immigrant steel workers. Per the FamilySearch wiki:

- **Pre-1906:** Naturalizations could be filed in **any court of record** — county courts, federal district courts, state courts. Allegheny County courthouse holds many declarations and petitions. The **National Archives at Philadelphia** holds federal court naturalizations.
- **Post-1906:** Centralized through federal courts; records held by NARA.
- **What they contain:** Declaration of intent ("first papers") and petition for naturalization ("second papers") — name, age, birthplace, port and date of arrival, sometimes occupation. Petition may name wife and children.
- **Access:** Many pre-1906 Pennsylvania naturalizations are on FamilySearch microfilm (browse-only); Ancestry has some indexed collections.

---

### Immigration Records

- **Primary ports used:** New York (Castle Garden through 1892, then Ellis Island from 1892) was the dominant arrival port for Eastern European and Southern European immigrants of the late 1800s. Philadelphia also served some arrivals.
- **Passenger lists:** At NARA; indexed + images on **Ancestry.com** and partially on **FamilySearch**. Lists name, age, sex, occupation, last residence, and destination.
- **Key strategy:** Work backward from the naturalization record (which gives arrival year and port) to locate the passenger list.

---

### Probate and Court Records

- **Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994** — 893,214 records, 893,214 persons, 446,607 images — **Indexed + images** on FamilySearch. Includes Allegheny County wills, estate inventories, and administration files.
- **Physical originals:** Allegheny County Register of Wills (courthouse) holds original probate files.
- **Note:** Many working-class steel workers died without wills; guardianship and administration records are often more useful than wills for this population.

---

### Military Records

- **Civil War veterans** were a significant portion of the Pittsburgh workforce in the 1870s–1880s. Many steel workers were veterans.
- **Relevant records:** Service and pension files at NARA (accessible on **Fold3** — subscription, or free at NARA facilities). Pennsylvania adjutant general records at the **Pennsylvania State Archives**.
- **1890 Veterans Schedule:** Lists surviving Union veterans and widows by household — partially compensates for the destroyed 1890 census.
- **Spanish-American War (1898):** Some younger steel workers served; NARA holds service records.

---

### Newspapers

- **Pittsburgh Post-Gazette** (founded 1786) and **Pittsburgh Press** are the major papers covering the full period. Available on **Newspapers.com** (subscription).
- **Ethnic newspapers:** Polish, Slovak, Croatian, and German-language papers served immigrant communities and published birth notices, obituaries, and marriage announcements in the heritage language. Locations vary — check the **Heinz History Center** collections.
- **Value for industrial research:** Newspapers covered the Homestead Strike (1892) and other labor actions in detail — can document specific workers, mill departments, and events. Also useful for obituaries that may name country of origin.

---

### Cemetery Records

- **FindAGrave.com** — free; covers major Allegheny County cemeteries including **Allegheny Cemetery**, **St. Mary's Cemetery**, and many ethnic Catholic cemeteries. Search by name.
- **BillionGraves** — another free cemetery transcription site with Allegheny County coverage.
- **Physical sexton records:** Some cemeteries hold original burial registers not fully digitized; contact individual cemetery offices.

---

### Land and Tax Records

- Pennsylvania is a **state-land state** (not federal land), so land patents derive from the Commonwealth, not the BLM GLO.
- **Deed records:** Allegheny County Recorder of Deeds (courthouse) holds original deeds. Some are microfilmed on FamilySearch.
- **Tax records:** Allegheny County assessment records can document property ownership and value annually — a useful annual substitute when census years don't align. Held at the county courthouse and state archives.

---

## Repository Guide

### Online Repositories

| Repository | Key Pittsburgh / Pennsylvania Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** | Pennsylvania Church & Town Records (1,245,830); County Marriages 1885–1950 (524,189); Probate Records 1683–1994 (893,214); Civil Marriages 1677–1950 (312,045); Death Certificates 1906–1968 (4,218,763); federal censuses 1860–1900 | Free |
| **Ancestry.com** | U.S. Federal Census 1860–1900; city directories; passenger lists; Pittsburgh newspapers; some naturalization indexes | Subscription |
| **Newspapers.com** | Pittsburgh Post-Gazette, Pittsburgh Press, and other regional papers | Subscription |
| **Fold3** | Civil War and Spanish-American War service and pension records | Subscription |
| **FindAGrave** | Allegheny County cemetery transcriptions | Free |
| **NARA (archives.gov)** | Federal naturalization petitions; passenger lists; military records | Free in-person; some online |

### Physical Repositories

| Repository | Key Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Senator John Heinz History Center** (Pittsburgh) | Industrial/employment records, immigration records, Heinz company records, HSWP library | In-person; contact for appointments |
| **Rivers of Steel National Heritage Area** (Homestead, PA) | Steel industry records, mill documentation, Homestead Strike archive | In-person; contact for appointments |
| **Carnegie Library of Pittsburgh** | City directories from 1810s, local history, Pittsburgh newspapers on microfilm | In-person; some digital |
| **Allegheny County Courthouse** | Deed books, naturalization records, probate files, marriage licenses | In-person |
| **Pennsylvania State Archives** (Harrisburg) | State factory inspector reports, state-level records, military records | In-person / written requests |
| **Diocese of Pittsburgh Archives** | Catholic parish registers not yet microfilmed | Written request / appointment |
| **National Archives at Philadelphia** (NARA Region 3) | Federal court naturalization records, federal census originals | In-person |

---

## Records NOT Online

The following records exist but are **not** available in any online database as of this survey:

- **Company employment and payroll records** (Carnegie Steel, Jones & Laughlin, etc.) — fragmentary; physical only at Heinz History Center and Rivers of Steel
- **Union membership records** (Amalgamated Association of Iron and Steel Workers) — physical only at Heinz History Center / HSWP
- **Pennsylvania State Factory Inspector Reports** — print documents at state archives and major libraries; not digitized
- **Many ethnic parish registers** not yet microfilmed or digitized — Diocese of Pittsburgh archives or individual parishes hold originals
- **Allegheny County tax assessment records** — physical at county courthouse

---

## Known Record Losses

- **1890 Federal Census:** Mostly destroyed in a 1921 fire in Washington, D.C. The 1890 Veterans Schedule partially compensates (Union veterans). **Substitute strategy:** Use the 1880 and 1900 censuses bracketing the gap; supplement with city directories (annual, available through the 1890s), church records, and tax records.
- **Pennsylvania vital registration before 1906:** No statewide system existed. Church records are the primary substitute for births and deaths.
- **Company records:** Many steel company records were lost or discarded during corporate mergers and closures across the 20th century. The surviving fraction at the Heinz History Center and Rivers of Steel is the extent of what is accessible.

---

## Research Tips

1. **Identify the ethnic parish first.** For immigrant steel workers before 1906, the ethnic Catholic or Lutheran parish is the single most valuable record source. The parish a family attended tells you their ethnic origin; once identified, you can target the right diocesan archive or FamilySearch microfilm reel.

2. **Search Allegheny City separately from Pittsburgh** before 1907. Ancestors living north of the Allegheny River were residents of the City of Allegheny (a separate municipality) until annexation in 1907. They appear in records as "Allegheny, Pennsylvania" — not Pittsburgh — despite being in the same county.

3. **Triangulate with city directories.** Because the 1890 census is destroyed and company records are fragmentary, Pittsburgh city directories published annually through the Carnegie Library are an irreplaceable tool. They list name, occupation, employer, and address every year — use them to track a family through the 1880s and 1890s.

4. **The Homestead Strike (1892) as a finding aid.** Congressional investigation records, newspaper coverage, and Pinkerton detective agency records from the Homestead Strike name individual workers. If your ancestor worked at Carnegie's Homestead mill, these may place him at a specific time and place.

5. **Pennsylvania naturalization before 1906 is scattered.** Check the Allegheny County courts, Pennsylvania state courts, and federal courts (NARA Philadelphia) — declarations and petitions could be filed in any court of record. A single family may have split their filings across different courts.

6. **Use the FamilySearch compiled marriages index (1700–1900) as a pointer only.** This collection has 198,765 records but **zero images** — it's a name index to original records you must then obtain from the county courthouse or microfilm.

7. **Factory inspector reports for industry context.** Even when your ancestor is not named individually, Pennsylvania's annual factory inspector reports confirm which mills operated in which neighborhoods, how many workers each employed, and what working conditions were like — essential context for interpreting other records.

8. **Contact Rivers of Steel directly.** This National Heritage Area organization is specifically focused on the steel industry's history and has archival holdings that are not catalogued in any online database. A direct inquiry describing your ancestor's likely employer and time period may surface records unavailable anywhere else.

---

# Locality Guide: Philadelphia, Pennsylvania — 1793 Yellow Fever Epidemic (c. 1780–1810)

## Jurisdiction overview

- **Formed:** 1682 by William Penn; both a city and a co-extensive county from founding
- **Administrative center:** Philadelphia (city and county seat are the same)
- **Parent jurisdiction:** Pennsylvania (Commonwealth)
- **Population during period:** Approximately 50,000 in 1793 (Wikipedia); the 1790 federal census and 1800 federal census bracket the epidemic year precisely
- **Economy:** Major Atlantic port; commerce, shipping, printing, crafts, and early manufacturing; seat of the federal government 1790–1800
- **Ethnic composition:** English Quakers, German Lutherans and Reformed, Scots-Irish Presbyterians, Swedish settlers (along the Delaware), Irish Catholics, free Black Philadelphians, and recent French Caribbean refugees who arrived just before the epidemic
- **Religious denominations:** Quaker (Friends), Anglican/Episcopal (Christ Church, St. Peter's), Presbyterian, Lutheran, German Reformed, Roman Catholic, Methodist, Baptist, Jewish (Congregation Mikveh Israel), and Gloria Dei (Old Swedes' Church, Swedish Lutheran, est. 1700)
- **Key historical events:** The 1793 yellow fever epidemic (August–November) killed approximately 5,000 people — roughly one-tenth of the city's population. Thousands fled. Those who stayed are documented in death and burial registers, newspaper death lists, the published roll of the dead by Mathew Carey, and the records of the citizen relief committee. Mayor Matthew Clarkson and physician Benjamin Rush left detailed contemporary accounts.

## Boundary changes

Philadelphia County and city were co-extensive from 1682. In 1854 the Act of Consolidation merged all townships and districts into the unified City of Philadelphia — but that is well after your period. For 1793, the jurisdiction is straightforward: a single city-county with no subdivision ambiguity.

---

## Available record types

### ⚠️ Epidemic-specific sources — START HERE

These sources name the dead and the living directly connected to the 1793 outbreak and are not available for any other period:

| Source | What it contains | Where held | Access |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mathew Carey's published rolls** | *A Short Account of the Malignant Fever* (1793, multiple editions) — includes a named list of the dead and prominent survivors | Digitized; available via Google Books, HathiTrust, and the Library Company of Philadelphia | Free online |
| **Committee of citizens' minutes** | The citizen relief committee (chaired by Mayor Clarkson) kept meeting minutes naming those who received aid, those who died, and those who fled | Historical Society of Pennsylvania (HSP), manuscript collection | Physical / in-person |
| **Bush Hill Hospital records** | Register of patients admitted to the fever hospital operated by Stephen Girard | Philadelphia City Archives; some material at HSP | Physical / in-person |
| **Newspaper death columns** | Philadelphia papers published daily death tolls **and individual names** during Aug–Nov 1793: *Pennsylvania Gazette*, *General Advertiser* (later *Aurora General Advertiser*), *Dunlap's American Daily Advertiser* | Newspapers.com (subscription); Library Company of Philadelphia (physical microfilm) | Subscription/physical |
| **Almshouse / Guardians of the Poor records** | Recorded pauper deaths, orphaned children placed after parents died of fever | Philadelphia City Archives | Physical / in-person |

---

### Vital records (civil registration)

- **Start date:** Pennsylvania did **not** require statewide civil registration of births, marriages, or deaths until **1906**. There are NO government-issued death certificates for 1793.
- **What exists for 1793:** Nothing in the civil registration system — church burial registers and the epidemic-specific sources above are the only contemporaneous death records.
- **FamilySearch collection:** *Pennsylvania, Death Certificates, 1906–1968* (collection 1921317) — 4,218,763 records, fully indexed with images — is entirely post-period and not useful for 1793 research directly. It may help you find later descendants.

### Church records — PRIMARY SOURCE FOR 1793 DEATHS

Church burial registers are the single most important record class for tracing 1793 deaths. The epidemic caused an extraordinary spike in burials across all denominations.

- **FamilySearch collection:** **Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985** (collection 1401638) — 1,245,830 records, 982,415 persons indexed, 607,220 images — **Indexed + images**. This is the largest searchable collection and should be your first stop after the epidemic-specific sources above.
- **Key Philadelphia congregations with burial records:**
  - **Christ Church (Anglican/Episcopal)** — one of the city's oldest, large membership; burial register covers the epidemic period; transcribed records available through the Genealogical Society of Pennsylvania (GSP)
  - **Gloria Dei / Old Swedes' Church (Swedish Lutheran)** — est. 1700; earliest extant church registers in the region; named explicitly in the FamilySearch Wiki Philadelphia genealogy article as a key source for the epidemic
  - **St. Peter's Church (Episcopal)** — burial register continuous through 1793
  - **First Presbyterian Church / Second Presbyterian** — active congregations with registers
  - **St. Mary's Catholic** — burial records for Catholic decedents
  - **Society of Friends (Quaker) Meeting records** — Quakers recorded deaths in monthly meeting minutes; Friends Historical Library at Swarthmore College holds the originals; many microfilmed by FamilySearch
  - **St. Michael's Lutheran / Zion Lutheran** — German-language registers; records may be in German
  - **Mikveh Israel** — records for Jewish decedents
- **Language note:** Lutheran and German Reformed records from this period may be in German — be prepared to read the originals or find translations.
- **Indexing note:** Not every Philadelphia congregation's registers are in the FamilySearch indexed collection. The **Genealogical Society of Pennsylvania** (GSP) has compiled additional unpublished indexes. Many original registers remain at individual churches or at the Philadelphia Archdiocesan Historical Research Center.

### Census records

| Census | Year | Notes | Access |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1790 federal | 1790 | Taken 3 years before the epidemic; establishes household composition | Indexed + images — FamilySearch (free), Ancestry (subscription) |
| 1800 federal | 1800 | Taken 7 years after; captures survivors and family reconstitution | Indexed + images — FamilySearch (free), Ancestry (subscription) |
| 1800 mortality schedule | N/A | Mortality schedules do not begin until 1850; there is no mortality schedule for 1793 |

**Research tip:** Compare 1790 and 1800 census entries for the same household. Heads who appear in 1790 but are absent in 1800 with a restructured household may have died during the epidemic.

### Probate and court records

The epidemic generated an extraordinary surge in estate filings in late 1793 and early 1794. This makes probate records a particularly rich resource for this period.

- **FamilySearch collection:** **Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994** (collection 1417683) — 893,214 records, indexed with images — **Indexed + images**
- **Types:** Wills, letters of administration, guardianship bonds, inventories, estate distributions
- **Where held originally:** Register of Wills, Philadelphia City Hall / City Archives
- **Research tip:** When an intestate decedent left minor children, a guardian was appointed — the guardianship bond names the children and their ages. This is especially valuable when the epidemic left orphans.

### Marriage records

- **Pennsylvania, Civil Marriages, 1677–1950** (collection 1571957) — 312,045 records, indexed with images — **Indexed + images**
- **Pennsylvania, Compiled Marriages, 1700–1900** (collection 1502909) — 198,765 records, **no images** (index only) — **Index only**
- Pre-1906 marriages were solemnized by churches or magistrates; church registers remain the primary source

### Land and tax records

- **System:** Metes and bounds (Pennsylvania is a state-land state — no BLM/GLO records)
- **Types:** Deed books (grantor/grantee), mortgage books, property tax records (Provincial Tax Records predate the Revolution; later Philadelphia County tax lists continue through the 1790s)
- **Tax lists are especially valuable** as a census substitute — Philadelphia County tax lists from the 1790s name heads of household with property values and can confirm residence or absence (flight from the city)
- **Where held:** Philadelphia City Archives (deed books, tax records); Pennsylvania State Archives (Harrisburg) holds some colonial-era records
- **Online access:** Philadelphia deeds are not comprehensively digitized for this period; physical visit or correspondence with Philadelphia City Archives is required

### Military records

- **Relevant conflict:** The Revolutionary War (ended 1783) is immediately prior; many 1793 residents were veterans
- **Record types:** Revolutionary War service records and pension files at NARA (Washington, DC and regional centers); Fold3 has digitized many pension files and service records — **Indexed + images** on Fold3 (subscription)
- **Militia:** Pennsylvania militia records for this period are at the Pennsylvania State Archives
- **Note:** The yellow fever epidemic intersected with military service in that many veterans died in the epidemic; pension widow's files filed after 1793 may describe the veteran's death

### Cemetery records

- **Philadelphia's major burial grounds in 1793:** Christ Church Burial Ground (5th & Arch Sts.), Gloria Dei churchyard, St. Peter's churchyard, Friends burial grounds (multiple), Laurel Hill Cemetery (est. 1836 — post-period for the epidemic itself), Potter's Field
- **Online coverage:** FindAGrave ([findagrave.com](https://www.findagrave.com)) has memorials for Philadelphia cemeteries, though coverage for colonial-era and early national burials is uneven — **Indexed (no images for most)**
- **Note:** The epidemic overwhelmed normal burial practices; many victims were buried hastily in common graves or in churchyards without individual headstones. Sexton records at Christ Church and Gloria Dei are more reliable than headstone evidence for this period

### Newspaper records

- **Key papers active in 1793:**
  - *Pennsylvania Gazette* — established 1728; published during the epidemic
  - *General Advertiser* — published named death lists during Aug–Nov 1793
  - *Dunlap's American Daily Advertiser*
- **Where held:** Newspapers.com (subscription) covers many Philadelphia papers; Library Company of Philadelphia holds the most complete physical run of Philadelphia newspapers and has a reading room
- **Content value for epidemic research:** Daily death lists named individuals; advertisements for missing persons (those who fled) ran throughout the epidemic months; probate and estate notices appeared afterward

### Immigration and emigration records

- **Philadelphia was the primary port of entry** for the mid-Atlantic region in this period
- **Record types:** NARA passenger arrival lists begin in earnest after 1820; pre-1820 lists are sparse but some survive — search NARA's series M237/M425
- **Relevant migration context:** French Caribbean refugees (from Saint-Domingue, fleeing revolution) arrived in Philadelphia in large numbers in 1793 — many were among the epidemic's victims. If your ancestor was French or Haitian, this migration corridor is important
- **Access:** NARA (free online at [archives.gov](https://www.archives.gov)); Ancestry (subscription)

### Tax records

- **Types:** Philadelphia County direct tax (1798 federal "glass and windows" tax — a snapshot of property), annual county tax lists
- **The 1798 Direct Tax** is especially useful — it lists every property owner and tenant in the city with a physical description of the dwelling, just 5 years after the epidemic
- **Where held:** NARA (1798 Direct Tax, indexed on Ancestry); Philadelphia City Archives (county tax lists)
- **Substitute value:** Tax lists from 1790–1800 serve as a de facto annual census, tracking who was present, absent, or deceased year by year

### Other record types — institutional and relief

- **Guardians of the Poor / Overseers of the Poor:** Recorded relief recipients, orphans, and pauper burials — enormously relevant for the epidemic's aftermath. Philadelphia City Archives.
- **Pennsylvania Hospital records:** Opened 1751; admission registers from the 1790s name patients. The Pennsylvania Hospital Historic Collections holds these.
- **Orphan Court records:** Guardianship and orphan's court proceedings for minor children whose parents died in the epidemic. Philadelphia City Archives / Register of Wills.

---

## Repository guide

### Online repositories

| Repository | Collections for Philadelphia c. 1793 | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** | Pennsylvania Church and Town Records 1708–1985 (1.2M records); Pennsylvania Probate Records 1683–1994 (893K records); Pennsylvania Civil Marriages 1677–1950; Pennsylvania Compiled Marriages 1700–1900; 1790 & 1800 federal census | Free |
| **Ancestry** | 1790 & 1800 federal census (indexed); 1798 Direct Tax; Philadelphia passenger lists; Fold3 military records via Ancestry | Subscription |
| **Fold3** | Revolutionary War pension files, service records, NARA-sourced military records | Subscription |
| **Newspapers.com** | Philadelphia newspapers including papers that published epidemic death lists | Subscription |
| **FindAGrave** | Philadelphia cemetery memorials (coverage uneven for 1793) | Free |
| **HathiTrust / Google Books** | Mathew Carey's *Short Account of the Malignant Fever* (1793) — digitized primary source naming the dead | Free |
| **NARA (archives.gov)** | Passenger lists, Revolutionary War records, 1798 Direct Tax | Free |

### Physical repositories

| Repository | Holdings relevant to 1793 epidemic research | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Philadelphia City Archives** | Deed books, tax lists, Guardians of the Poor records, Bush Hill / almshouse records, Committee of citizens' records, orphan's court | In-person; some records available by mail request |
| **Historical Society of Pennsylvania (HSP)** | Manuscript sources for the epidemic: Committee minutes, Benjamin Rush papers, contemporary diaries and accounts; many church record transcriptions | In-person reading room (Philadelphia) |
| **Genealogical Society of Pennsylvania (GSP)** | Unpublished indexes to Philadelphia church records; compiled genealogies; city directory indexes | In-person / some digital via HSP partnership |
| **Library Company of Philadelphia** | Nearly complete run of 1793 Philadelphia newspapers; Mathew Carey papers; colonial and early national imprints | In-person reading room (Philadelphia) |
| **Pennsylvania State Archives (Harrisburg)** | Colonial and early state records; militia records; land warrants | In-person; some records available online |
| **Friends Historical Library, Swarthmore College** | Quaker monthly meeting records including death minutes for the 1793 period | In-person; some microfilmed |
| **Philadelphia Archdiocesan Historical Research Center** | Catholic parish records for St. Mary's and other Catholic congregations | In-person by appointment |
| **Pennsylvania Hospital Historic Collections** | Hospital admission registers from the 1790s | In-person by appointment |
| **NARA Mid-Atlantic Region (Philadelphia)** | Federal court records, passenger lists, naturalization records | In-person |

---

## Records NOT online

The following records exist and are genealogically significant for 1793 Philadelphia research but are **not** available through any online database — you must visit or write to the holding repository:

- **Committee of citizens' manuscript minutes** (HSP) — names of those who received relief
- **Bush Hill Hospital patient registers** (Philadelphia City Archives / HSP)
- **Almshouse / Guardians of the Poor admission and burial registers** (Philadelphia City Archives)
- **Philadelphia County annual tax lists, 1790s** (Philadelphia City Archives) — not the same as the 1798 federal direct tax, which is digitized
- **Most individual church burial registers** not yet in the FamilySearch indexed collection — originals remain at individual churches or diocesan archives
- **Orphan's Court / guardianship bonds** created after the epidemic (Philadelphia City Archives)
- **Pennsylvania Hospital admission registers, 1790s** (Pennsylvania Hospital Historic Collections)

---

## Known record considerations

- **No civil death registration in 1793.** Pennsylvania required no death registration until 1906. Church burial registers and the epidemic-specific sources are the only contemporaneous death records.
- **Epidemic disrupted normal record-keeping.** Some churches suspended normal register entry during the worst months (August–October 1793) and made entries retrospectively — handwriting and detail may be thinner than usual.
- **Flight from the city.** Thousands left Philadelphia during the epidemic. Persons who appear in 1790 Philadelphia records but not in 1793 church records may have survived by fleeing — look for them in surrounding counties (Bucks, Montgomery, Delaware, Chester) during the epidemic months.
- **French Caribbean refugees.** A significant wave of Saint-Domingue refugees arrived in 1793. Many did not appear in 1790 Philadelphia census and were disproportionately affected by the epidemic.

---

## Research tips

1. **Start with Mathew Carey's named list of the dead** (free on HathiTrust/Google Books) — it is the only near-contemporaneous compiled roll of victims and is immediately searchable.
2. **Search the FamilySearch *Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records* collection first** for burial entries; then go to the individual church archives for any congregation not represented there.
3. **Compare 1790 and 1800 census entries** — a missing household head in 1800 often signals an epidemic death; look for the widow's remarriage or orphaned children placed with relatives.
4. **Check probate records for late 1793 and early 1794** — the epidemic generated a massive spike in estate filings. Even modest estates went through administration. The Philadelphia probate records on FamilySearch (collection 1417683) are indexed and searchable by year.
5. **Search Philadelphia newspapers (Aug–Nov 1793)** on Newspapers.com for daily death columns that named victims — and for notices placed by survivors looking for missing family members.
6. **For German or Lutheran families**, be prepared to read German-language church registers or engage a translator; Zion Lutheran and St. Michael's records are in German.
7. **For Quaker families**, the monthly meeting death minutes (Friends Historical Library, Swarthmore) are more detailed than most church burial registers and may include cause of death.
8. **The 1798 federal Direct Tax** (5 years post-epidemic) is a powerful follow-up tool — it lists every property owner and tenant in Philadelphia with a physical description of the property, letting you trace which families remained in the city and where.
9. **Philadelphia City Archives is essential** for any serious research on this period — their holdings of almshouse, committee, and institutional records are not replicated online.
10. The **FamilySearch Wiki article "Philadelphia Genealogy"** (at familysearch.org/en/wiki/Philadelphia_Genealogy) contains a dedicated section on the 1793 yellow fever epidemic with additional source guidance.
